1:
"Now there were in the church that was at Antioch certain prophets and teachers; as Barnabas, and Simeon that was called Niger, and Lucius of Cyrene, and Manaen, which had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul." The witnesses note the gifted leadership of the church at Antioch from which the first missionary journey would spring. The grammatical voices note the five named men — prophets and teachers — a richly endowed church, the first to be called Christian (11:26).

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the diversity of the company: Barnabas the Cypriot Levite; Simeon called Niger (the black); Lucius of Cyrene; Manaen, the foster-brother of Herod the tetrarch; and Saul. A varied group of gifted men, of different nations and backgrounds, united in the ministry of the word.

The witnesses note the significance of Antioch as the cradle of the Gentile mission. From this church, gifted with prophets and teachers, the Spirit would send forth Barnabas and Saul (v.2) to carry the gospel to the Gentile world. Manaen's connection with Herod's household shows the gospel's reach even into high circles.

The thin comment matches the verse, which opens the great chapter of the first missionary journey. The witnesses agree: the church at Antioch had a gifted leadership of prophets and teachers — Barnabas, Simeon Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen (Herod's foster-brother), and Saul — a varied company of different nations and backgrounds, united in the ministry of the word (Henry). The first church to be called Christian (11:26) becomes the cradle of the Gentile mission. The verse displays the rich endowment of the Antioch church: men of prophecy and teaching, from whom the Spirit would send forth Barnabas and Saul to the Gentiles. The verse marks the beginning of the deliberate, organized mission to the Gentile world — the great step that would carry the gospel through Cyprus, Asia Minor, and beyond. The diversity of the named leaders foreshadows the universal reach of the gospel they would proclaim. From this gifted and varied church, the Spirit would call out the first missionaries, opening the era of the Gentile mission that would dominate the rest of Acts. Antioch, not Jerusalem, becomes the base from which the gospel goes forth to the nations.
2:
"As they ministered to the Lord, and fasted, the Holy Ghost said, Separate me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them." The witnesses note the Spirit's call to the first mission and its testimony to his deity. On "ministered to the Lord," the grammatical voices note the word (leitourgeo) used of priestly service. Robertson: "Here of worship (prayer, exhortation, fasting). The word liturgy grows out of this use." They were engaged in public worship and fasting when the Spirit spoke.

On "Separate me," Barnes clarifies its meaning: "It does not mean to ordain, but simply to designate, or appoint to this specific work... not the apostolic office... but was a temporary designation to a missionary enterprise." The Spirit set apart Barnabas and Saul for the specific work of the Gentile mission, to which he had already called them.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the testimony to the Spirit's deity: "the Spirit challengeth this right when he commandeth that Paul and Barnabas be separated to him, and testifieth that they were called by his beck... we gather that the Holy Ghost is God indeed, whose authority is sufficient to choose pastors." He notes against Sabellius that the Spirit is "a person truly subsisting in God," for it would be absurd to say a bare epithet "said" this.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as the prophets ministered (in priestly worship, Robertson) and fasted, the Holy Spirit said "Separate me Barnabas and Saul" — designating them, not ordaining, for the specific work of the Gentile mission to which he had already called them (Barnes). Calvin draws the testimony to the Spirit's deity: the Spirit claims the right to choose ministers and call them by his own authority, proving he is God — and, against Sabellius, a true person, for a bare epithet could not "say" this. The verse displays the divine initiative in the Gentile mission: the Spirit himself calls and sets apart the missionaries. The verse marks the formal commencement of the organized mission to the Gentiles: not a human plan but the Spirit's command, given to a praying, fasting church. The Spirit who calls is God, sovereign over the church's ministry. The verse establishes the divine authority behind the first missionary journey — Barnabas and Saul sent not by men but by the Holy Spirit, who chooses, calls, and commissions his servants for the work of the gospel among the nations.
3:
"And when they had fasted and prayed, and laid their hands on them, they sent them away." The witnesses note the solemn sending of the first missionaries. On the fasting and prayer, Barnes notes the solemnity: "The first formal mission to the Gentiles was an important event in the church; and they engaged in this appointment with deep solemnity, and with humbling themselves before God." The church sought God's blessing on this new and perilous enterprise.

On the laying on of hands, the witnesses note it was not ordination to the ministry. Barnes: "This was not to set them apart to the apostolic office... Nor is it meant that this was an ordination to the ministry... For both had been engaged in this before... but was a designation to a particular field of labour." Robertson: "Not ordination to the ministry, but a solemn consecration to the great missionary task." Wesley: "This was not ordaining them... it was only inducting him to the province."

The Reformed Calvin notes the public fast and the meaning of the rite: "the laying on of hands was a kind of consecration... that the Church might offer them to God, and that they might with their consent declare that this office was enjoined them by God; for the calling was properly God's alone, but the external ordaining did belong to the Church."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the church, with fasting and prayer, laid hands on Barnabas and Saul and sent them away — a solemn sending of the first missionaries, the church humbling itself and seeking God's blessing on a new and perilous enterprise (Barnes). The laying on of hands was not ordination to the ministry (both had long preached) but a consecration to the specific missionary task (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley): the church offering them to God and declaring the work enjoined by him, the calling being God's alone but the external setting-apart the church's (Calvin). The verse displays the solemn commissioning of the first missionary journey: the church, in prayer and fasting, sets apart its best men for the Gentile mission. The verse marks the pattern of the church's sending of missionaries: the divine call recognized and confirmed by the church's prayer and the laying on of hands. The first formal mission to the Gentiles begins not casually but with deep solemnity, the church seeking God's blessing. The God who called Barnabas and Saul (v.2) now sends them forth through the prayerful consecration of the church — the divine call and the church's commission joined in the launching of the gospel's advance to the nations.
4:
"So they, being sent forth by the Holy Ghost, departed unto Seleucia; and from thence they sailed to Cyprus." The witnesses note the beginning of the journey and its divine direction. Chrysostom and the witnesses stress that they were "sent forth by the Holy Ghost" — the church's sending (v.3) was the Spirit's sending. The missionaries went not on their own initiative but as the Spirit's instruments.

On the route, the grammatical voices note Seleucia (the port of Antioch, on the Orontes) and Cyprus (Barnabas's native island, 4:36). The journey began naturally with Cyprus, where Barnabas had connections and where there was a Jewish population.

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the providential ordering of the mission. The Spirit who called them now directs their steps, beginning at Cyprus. The double emphasis — sent by the church (v.3) and by the Spirit (v.4) — shows the harmony of the divine call and the human commission.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Barnabas and Saul, "sent forth by the Holy Ghost," departed to Seleucia (the port of Antioch) and sailed to Cyprus (Barnabas's native island, 4:36) — the church's sending (v.3) being the Spirit's sending (Chrysostom). The journey began naturally with Cyprus, where Barnabas had connections and a Jewish population existed. The verse displays the divine direction of the mission: the Spirit who called the missionaries now directs their steps. The double emphasis — sent by the church and by the Spirit — shows the harmony of the divine call and the human commission. The verse marks the actual commencement of the first missionary journey: the missionaries embarking for Cyprus, the gospel's advance to the islands and the nations now begun. The God who called Barnabas and Saul guides their journey, beginning at the island of Barnabas's birth. The verse, brief as it is, launches the great enterprise that would carry the gospel through the Roman world — the Spirit-sent missionaries setting out on the path that Paul would follow to the ends of the earth. The first stage, Cyprus, opens the long record of the gospel's missionary advance.
5:
"And when they were at Salamis, they preached the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews: and they had also John to their minister." The witnesses note the apostolic method — preaching first in the synagogues — and the role of John Mark. On preaching to the Jews first, Robertson notes Paul's rule: "to the Jew first" (Romans 1:16; Acts 13:46). Calvin defends it: though sent to the Gentiles, "it was meet that they should begin with the Jews," who had the synagogues as a base.

On the synagogues, the witnesses note the considerable Jewish population at Salamis (the chief city of Cyprus). The missionaries followed their regular method: entering the synagogues, where Jews and God-fearing Gentiles gathered, and preaching the word there.

On John Mark as "their minister," the grammatical voices note the term (hyperetes, attendant). Ellicott: "the attendance of personal service... It is probable that he was employed in baptising converts, and, where a church was founded, in preparing for the Supper of the Lord." Robertson: "as their attendant... It is not clear what John Mark did." Calvin notes his help in the gospel work, "which made his later desertion the less excusable."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: at Salamis, the missionaries preached in the synagogues of the Jews — following Paul's rule of "to the Jew first" (Robertson, Romans 1:16), the synagogues serving as their base (Calvin) — with John Mark as their attendant (hyperetes), helping in the work (perhaps baptizing converts, Ellicott). The verse displays the apostolic method: preaching first to the Jews in the synagogues, where Jews and God-fearers gathered, before turning to the Gentiles. The verse marks the beginning of the Cyprus mission's work: the word of God preached in the synagogues of Salamis. The presence of John Mark as minister sets up the later breach (vv.13, 15:38) when he would desert the work. The verse establishes the pattern that Paul would follow throughout his journeys: entering the synagogue first, preaching Christ to the Jews and God-fearing Gentiles, and from that base extending the gospel to the wider Gentile world. The mission proceeds in order — to the Jew first, then to the Gentile — and the word of God is preached in the synagogues of Cyprus.
6:
"And when they had gone through the isle unto Paphos, they found a certain sorcerer, a false prophet, a Jew, whose name was Bar-jesus:" The witnesses note the journey across Cyprus and the encounter with the sorcerer. The grammatical voices note they crossed the whole island from Salamis (east) to Paphos (west), the seat of the proconsul. Barnes: "Paphos was a city at the western extremity of the island... the residence of the proconsul, and was distinguished for a splendid temple erected to Venus."

On Bar-jesus, the witnesses note he was "a sorcerer, a false prophet, a Jew." Robertson: "These Jewish mountebanks were numerous and had great influence with the uneducated." Ellicott: "The lower class of Jews here seem to have been specially addicted to such practices. They traded on the religious prestige of their race." A Jewish magician and false prophet, exploiting the credulity of the Gentiles.

The Reformed Calvin marks the goodness of God in bringing the gospel to such a place: "though all the island was dedicated to Venus, yet Paphos was the principal seat of the idol. For which cause the goodness of God is more wonderful, in that he would have the light of his gospel to pierce into such a filthy and cruel den."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the missionaries crossed the whole island to Paphos (the western seat of the proconsul, famed for the temple of Venus, Barnes) and found Bar-jesus, a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet — one of the numerous Jewish magicians who exploited the credulity of the Gentiles, trading on the prestige of their race (Robertson, Ellicott). Calvin marks the goodness of God in bringing the gospel even to Paphos, the filthy seat of idolatry. The verse displays the confrontation that the mission would face: the gospel meeting the entrenched powers of sorcery and false religion. The verse introduces the great encounter at Paphos (vv.7-12): the apostles versus the Jewish sorcerer, the truth of God versus the lie of magic. The setting — the idolatrous city of Venus, with its resident magician — magnifies the triumph of the gospel that would follow. The verse marks the first great test of the mission: the confrontation with Elymas, the false prophet who would seek to turn the proconsul from the faith (v.8), and whom Paul would strike blind (v.11). The light of the gospel pierces the den of idolatry and magic.
7:
"Which was with the deputy of the country, Sergius Paulus, a prudent man; who called for Barnabas and Saul, and desired to hear the word of God." The witnesses note the proconsul Sergius Paulus and his desire to hear — and address Luke's historical accuracy. On "deputy" (proconsul), the grammatical voices defend Luke's precision. Robertson: "Luke used to be sharply criticized for applying this term... That was true B.C. 30, but five years later it was changed to proconsul by Augustus and put under the control of the Senate. Two inscriptions have been found... one with the name of Paulus as Proconsul, undoubtedly this very man." Ellicott concurs, citing the coins and inscriptions.

On "a prudent man," the witnesses note his intelligence and openness. Barnes: "intelligent, wise, learned... of a philosophic and inquiring turn of mind, and was willing to obtain knowledge from any source." Clarke: "a man of good sense... he did not persecute the men for their preaching, but sent for them that he might hear for himself."

The Reformed Calvin reads his seeking as a providential preparation: "he sendeth for Paul and Barnabas of his own accord, to teach him. Therefore, he had conceived a certain reverence and fear of God, though he knew him not as yet." Weary of superstition, he desired a purer knowledge of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proconsul Sergius Paulus, a prudent man, called for Barnabas and Saul, desiring to hear the word of God. Luke's term "deputy" (proconsul) is historically precise — Cyprus having been transferred to senatorial control under Augustus, with inscriptions and coins confirming a proconsul Paulus (Robertson, Ellicott), vindicating Luke against the charge of error. The proconsul's intelligence and openness (Barnes, Clarke) led him to seek the word, perhaps weary of superstition and desiring a purer knowledge of God (Calvin). The verse displays both the accuracy of Luke as a historian and the providential seeking of the proconsul. The verse marks the opening of the great encounter at Paphos: a high Roman official, intelligent and open, desiring to hear the gospel — and a Jewish sorcerer seeking to prevent him (v.8). The conversion of so distinguished a man would be a signal triumph of the mission. The verse also stands as a notable confirmation of Luke's precision: the "deputy" is exactly the right title, attested by archaeology — one of the many marks of the historian's accuracy that commend the trustworthiness of Acts.
8:
"But Elymas the sorcerer (for so is his name by interpretation) withstood them, seeking to turn away the deputy from the faith." The witnesses note the sorcerer's opposition. On the name "Elymas," the grammatical voices note it is the interpretation of his title, not of Bar-jesus. Barnes: "Elymas is the interpretation, not of the name Bar-jesus, but of the word rendered the sorcerer. It is an Arabic word." Ellicott connects it to the Arabic Ulema (sage), "a title describing his claims to wisdom and supernatural powers."

On his opposition, the witnesses note his self-interest. Barnes: "He was sensible that if the influence of Saul and Barnabas should be extended over the proconsul, that he would be seen to be an impostor, and his power be at an end. His interest, therefore, led him to oppose the gospel." Robertson: "He persisted in his opposition and was unwilling to lose his great prize."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on Satan's opposition to the light: "No marvel if the seducer seek to put away the light, whereby he saw his own darkness driven away... The same struggle have we at this day with a number of babblers, who use to sell their smokes, and to shut, by all shifts possible, the eyes of the simple."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Elymas the sorcerer (the title interpreting his claim to wisdom, Barnes, Ellicott) withstood the apostles, seeking to turn the proconsul from the faith — moved by self-interest, fearing the loss of his influence over his patron (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin draws the lesson on Satan's opposition to the light: the seducer seeks to put away the gospel that exposes his darkness, as false teachers in every age shut the eyes of the simple. The verse displays the conflict at the heart of the mission: the gospel meeting the resistance of entrenched falsehood. Elymas, fearing for his power, opposes the apostles and seeks to keep the proconsul in his snare. The verse sets up the decisive confrontation (vv.9-11): Paul, filled with the Spirit, would denounce and strike blind the sorcerer who opposed the truth. The verse marks the pattern of opposition the gospel would face — not only from Jewish unbelief but from the powers of magic and false religion that profited from men's credulity. The sorcerer's resistance, motivated by self-interest, is the type of all who oppose the gospel because it threatens their power and gain.
9:
"Then Saul, (who also is called Paul,) filled with the Holy Ghost, set his eyes on him," The witnesses note the first use of the name Paul and his Spirit-filled confrontation. On the name change, the witnesses divide. Ellicott connects it to the conversion of Sergius Paulus: "It is impossible not to connect the mention, and probably the assumption, of the new name with the conversion of the proconsul... It formed a link between him and the illustrious convert." Robertson rejects this as "an element of vulgarity impossible to St. Paul," holding he "had both names all the time" as a Jew and Roman citizen, Luke now using the Gentile name as Paul turns to the Gentiles. Barnes and Wesley incline to its first use here, given by the Romans.

On "filled with the Holy Ghost," the grammatical voices note the sudden access of power. Ellicott: "a sudden access of spiritual power, showing itself at once in insight into character, righteous indignation, and prevision of the divine chastisement." Robertson: "A special influx of power to meet this emergency."

On "set his eyes on him," the witnesses note the fixed gaze (atenizo), possibly connected to Paul's weak eyesight (Ellicott), the intense look of one filled with the Spirit before the confrontation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: here first the name "Paul" is used, and the apostle, "filled with the Holy Ghost," set his eyes on the sorcerer. On the name, the witnesses divide: Ellicott connects its assumption to the conversion of Sergius Paulus (a link with the convert); Robertson rejects this as unworthy, holding Paul always had both names (Jew and Roman citizen), Luke now using the Gentile name as Paul turns to the Gentiles; Barnes and Wesley incline to its first use here, given by the Romans. The "filling with the Spirit" was a sudden access of power — insight, indignation, and prevision of judgment (Ellicott, Robertson). The verse displays the transition that marks the rest of Acts: Saul becomes Paul, the apostle to the Gentiles, here taking the lead. The verse marks a turning-point: from this point Paul (not Saul) is the name used, and Paul (not Barnabas) takes the leadership. Filled with the Spirit, Paul confronts the sorcerer — the Spirit-empowered apostle versus the agent of Satan. The name change, however explained, signals the new era of the Gentile mission, in which Paul, the apostle to the nations, stands at the center. The verse opens the decisive confrontation in which the power of God in Paul would overcome the power of magic in Elymas.
10:
"And said, O full of all subtilty and all mischief, thou child of the devil, thou enemy of all righteousness, wilt thou not cease to pervert the right ways of the Lord?" The witnesses note Paul's Spirit-inspired denunciation of the sorcerer. The grammatical voices note the force of the indictment. Robertson: "With deadly accuracy Paul pictured this slick rascal... Note triple use of 'all,' total depravity in every sense." The threefold "all" — subtilty, mischief, righteousness opposed — sums up the sorcerer's character.

On "child of the devil," the witnesses note the contrast with his name. Ellicott: "There is, perhaps, an intentional contrast between the meaning of the name Bar-jesus (= son of the Lord who saves) and the character of the man." Robertson: "a slanderer like the diabolos." The "son of Jesus" is in truth a son of the devil.

The Reformed Calvin defends the vehemence as Spirit-led: "lest any man should think that Paul was out of measure angry, Luke saith plainly that the inspiration of the Spirit was his guide... Thus must we deal with the desperate enemies of the gospel, in whom appeareth open contumacy and wicked contempt of God, especially when they stop the way before others." Yet he warns teachers to guard against fleshly anger under colour of zeal.

The witnesses agree: Paul, filled with the Spirit, denounced the sorcerer — "full of all subtilty and mischief, child of the devil, enemy of all righteousness" — a Spirit-inspired indictment of devastating accuracy (Robertson), the threefold "all" summing up his total depravity. The "child of the devil" stands in pointed contrast to his name Bar-jesus ("son of Jesus," Ellicott): the supposed son of salvation is in truth a son of the slanderer. Calvin defends the vehemence as Spirit-led, the proper way to deal with the desperate enemies of the gospel who stop the way before others — yet warns teachers against fleshly anger under colour of zeal. The verse displays the righteous indignation of the Spirit-filled apostle against the agent of Satan who perverted the right ways of the Lord. The verse marks the confrontation of the gospel with entrenched falsehood: Paul, not by his own anger but by the Spirit's inspiration, exposes and denounces the sorcerer who fought against the truth. The denunciation, severe but just and Spirit-led, is the model of holy zeal against the open enemies of God — distinguished from the fleshly anger that Calvin warns against. The "enemy of all righteousness," perverting the straight ways of the Lord, is exposed by the power of God in Paul.
11:
"And now, behold, the hand of the Lord is upon thee, and thou shalt be blind, not seeing the sun for a season. And immediately there fell on him a mist and a darkness; and he went about seeking some to lead him by the hand." The witnesses note the judicial blindness inflicted on the sorcerer. On "the hand of the Lord," the grammatical voices note it denotes a divine chastisement. Robertson: "when God's hand touches one's life it may be in judgment as here with Elymas." The blindness was a miraculous judgment, evidence of Paul's inspiration.

On the fitness of the punishment, the witnesses note its moral aptness. Furneaux (cited by Robertson): "blindness for blindness, darkness without for wilful darkness within." Ellicott: "there was a moral fitness in blindness as the penalty of the sin the very essence of which was that the man was fighting against light." The sorcerer who darkened others is himself cast into darkness.

On "for a season," the witnesses note the mercy in the judgment. Barnes: "it was in mercy ordained that the blindness should not be permanent... Nothing would be more likely to lead him to reflection and repentance." There is an early tradition (Origen, Chrysostom) that Elymas was converted. Ellicott connects the blindness to Paul's own (9:8): Paul may have hoped it would lead Elymas, as his own blindness led him, to the Sun of Righteousness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul pronounced the judgment — "the hand of the Lord is upon thee, and thou shalt be blind for a season" — and immediately a mist and darkness fell on the sorcerer. The blindness was a miraculous divine judgment (Robertson), evidence of Paul's inspiration, and morally fitting: blindness for the man who fought against light and darkened others (Furneaux, Ellicott). Yet "for a season" marks the mercy in it — designed to lead him to repentance (Barnes), and there is a tradition of his conversion; Paul, recalling his own blindness (9:8), may have hoped it would lead Elymas to the light. The verse displays the power of God in the apostle and the justice of the judgment: the sorcerer who darkened the proconsul's mind is himself struck blind, groping for a guide. The verse marks the triumph of the gospel over magic: the agent of Satan overcome by the power of God in Paul. The judicial blindness, both just and merciful, vindicates the truth and (it is hoped) leads the sorcerer to repentance. The man who led others into darkness now gropes in darkness himself — the just and remedial hand of the Lord upon the enemy of righteousness.
12:
"Then the deputy, when he saw what was done, believed, being astonished at the doctrine of the Lord." The witnesses note the conversion of the proconsul. On the cause of his faith, the witnesses note both the miracle and the doctrine. Robertson: "it was the teaching about the Lord by which he was astonished, as well as by the miracle." The blindness of Elymas removed the obstacle; the doctrine of the Lord convinced him.

On the genuineness of his faith, the witnesses defend it against skepticism. Barnes: "There seems no reason to doubt that his faith was that which is connected with eternal life... it is an evidence that the gospel was not always confined to the poor." Robertson defends it against Renan's skepticism: "I do not see that Luke leaves him in the category of Simon Magus who believed for revenue only."

The Reformed Calvin notes the miracle as the door to faith: "he was brought by the miracle unto faith, because the reverence of doctrine is the beginning of faith, and the preparation. Therefore, forasmuch as he saw an evident token of the power of God, he knew that Paul was sent of God." The miracle broke the snare of the sorcerer and opened his heart to the doctrine.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the proconsul, seeing the judgment on Elymas, believed, astonished at the doctrine of the Lord — convinced both by the miracle (which removed the sorcerer's obstacle and proved Paul sent of God) and by the teaching itself (Robertson, Calvin). The witnesses defend the genuineness of his faith against skepticism: it was true, saving faith, evidence that the gospel reached even the great and intelligent (Barnes), not the spurious belief of Simon Magus (Robertson, against Renan). The verse displays the fruit of the confrontation: the conversion of a distinguished Roman official, the first such recorded in Acts. The verse marks the triumph of the gospel at Paphos: the sorcerer struck blind, the proconsul converted — the power of God overcoming the power of magic, and the truth winning a notable soul. The conversion of Sergius Paulus shows that the gospel is for all ranks, the wise and the great as well as the poor and obscure. The miracle that blinded the enemy opened the eyes of the seeker: the proconsul, freed from the sorcerer's snare and convinced by the doctrine of the Lord, believed — the firstfruits of the Gentile mission in its full reach.
13:
"Now when Paul and his company loosed from Paphos, they came to Perga in Pamphylia: and John departing from them returned to Jerusalem." The witnesses note Paul's emerging leadership and the departure of John Mark. On "Paul and his company," the grammatical voices note the new phrasing. Ellicott: "those about Paul... indicating the new position which from this time the Apostle began to occupy as the leader of the mission." Clarke: from this point Luke names Paul first; Robertson notes Barnabas's greatness in accepting the secondary position.

On John Mark's departure, the witnesses note the cause is unknown but blameworthy. Barnes: "Why he departed from them is unknown... But it is plain, from Acts 15:38, that it was from some cause which was deemed blameworthy." Various conjectures: fear of the perils ahead (Robertson, Ellicott), homesickness or affection for his mother (Clarke, Ellicott), or unwillingness to accept Paul's new leadership (Robertson). Ramsay suggests malaria at Perga.

The departure the witnesses note as the seed of the later breach. Acts 15:38: Paul would refuse to take Mark on the next journey because of this desertion, leading to the separation of Paul and Barnabas. Calvin notes Luke records it "because it was afterwards a cause of doleful disagreement."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and his company sailed from Paphos to Perga, and John Mark departed and returned to Jerusalem. The phrase "Paul and his company" marks Paul's emerging leadership (from this point Luke names him first, Barnabas graciously accepting the secondary place, Robertson, Ellicott). John Mark's departure, for reasons unknown but deemed blameworthy (Barnes, Acts 15:38) — whether fear of the perils ahead, affection for his mother, or unwillingness to accept Paul's leadership (Robertson, Ellicott) — would be the seed of the later breach between Paul and Barnabas (15:38). The verse displays both the rise of Paul to leadership and the human frailty that marred the mission. The verse marks a turning-point in the relationships of the mission: Paul now leads, and John Mark's desertion sows the seed of future disagreement. The verse records honestly the weakness in the company even amid the gospel's advance — the historian's candour in noting both the triumph and the failure. Paul presses on to Antioch of Pisidia; Mark turns back; and the mission continues under Paul's leadership, toward the great sermon that would follow.
14:
"But when they departed from Perga, they came to Antioch in Pisidia, and went into the synagogue on the sabbath day, and sat down." The witnesses note the journey to Antioch of Pisidia and the entrance into the synagogue. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the apostles' regular method: going to the synagogue on the sabbath, the natural place to find Jews and God-fearing Gentiles, and to preach Christ from the Scriptures.

The grammatical voices note Antioch of Pisidia (distinct from Syrian Antioch) was an inland city in the highlands of Asia Minor. The missionaries, having left the coast, pressed inland to this important center, with its Jewish community and synagogue.

On "sat down," the witnesses note the posture. The apostles entered and took their seats, as was customary, awaiting the reading of the Scriptures and the opportunity to speak. The synagogue provided the platform for the great sermon that would follow (vv.16-41).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the Antioch of Pisidia narrative. The witnesses agree: the missionaries journeyed from Perga to Antioch of Pisidia (an inland highland city, distinct from Syrian Antioch) and entered the synagogue on the sabbath, taking their seats — following their regular method of seeking the synagogue, where Jews and God-fearers gathered (Henry). The verse displays the apostolic strategy: using the synagogue as the base for preaching Christ, entering on the sabbath and awaiting the opportunity to speak. The verse marks the setting for Paul's first recorded full sermon (vv.16-41): the synagogue at Antioch of Pisidia, on the sabbath, where the reading of the law and prophets would lead to the invitation for Paul to address the people (v.15). The verse, brief as it is, sets the stage for the great discourse in which Paul would rehearse Israel's history and proclaim Jesus as the promised Savior. The missionaries, pressing inland to an important center, enter the synagogue — the natural starting-point for the gospel among a people who had the Scriptures and awaited the Messiah. The platform is set for the proclamation of Christ to Jews and God-fearing Gentiles alike.
15:
"And after the reading of the law and the prophets the rulers of the synagogue sent unto them, saying, Ye men and brethren, if ye have any word of exhortation for the people, say on." The witnesses note the synagogue service and the invitation to speak. The grammatical voices describe the order. Robertson: "There was a reading from the law and one from the prophets in Hebrew which was interpreted... The reading was followed by the sermon." Any distinguished visitor might be invited to speak (Robertson, Ellicott).

Ellicott notes a remarkable coincidence fixing the sabbath: Paul's opening words echo Deuteronomy 1 (the lesson for the forty-fourth sabbath) and Isaiah 1 (the corresponding prophetic lesson), from which he quotes — "He starts, as was natural, from what the people had just been listening to, as the text of his discourse."

On "word of exhortation," Clarke notes it may carry the sense of "consolation," the Messiah being "the Consolation of Israel": "they wished for consolation, and he declared unto them glad tidings." Calvin notes the office of the teacher: not to utter new things of his own but "to apply the Scripture... unto the present use of the people."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after the reading of the law and the prophets, the synagogue rulers invited any "word of exhortation" — the customary invitation to a distinguished visitor to address the people (Robertson, Ellicott). Ellicott notes the coincidence fixing the sabbath: Paul's sermon echoes the lessons just read (Deuteronomy 1 and Isaiah 1), starting from the text the people had heard. The "word of exhortation" may carry the sense of consolation (Clarke), the Messiah being the Consolation of Israel; and the teacher's office is to apply the Scripture to the people's present use (Calvin). The verse displays the providential opening for the gospel: the synagogue's own custom gave Paul the platform to preach Christ. The verse marks the invitation that launched Paul's great sermon (vv.16-41): the rulers, seeing distinguished visitors, asked them to speak, and Paul rose to proclaim Jesus as the promised Savior. The synagogue service — the reading of Scripture followed by exhortation — became the occasion for the gospel. The verse displays how the gospel advanced through the existing structures of Jewish worship: the reading of the law and prophets leading naturally to the proclamation of their fulfillment in Christ.
16:
"Then Paul stood up, and beckoning with his hand said, Men of Israel, and ye that fear God, give audience." The witnesses note the opening of Paul's great sermon. Chrysostom marks Paul's tactful address: "having first praised them, and showed his great regard for them in the words, 'ye that fear God,' he so begins his discourse." Paul addresses two classes — the Jews ("Men of Israel") and the God-fearing Gentiles ("ye that fear God").

On the gesture, the grammatical voices note "beckoning with his hand" (Robertson: a dramatic gesture for quiet, as in 12:17; 21:40), and "stood up" (Robertson: possibly the Greek custom, or stepping onto the platform). Ellicott notes the graphic touches suggest an eyewitness report.

On the significance, Robertson notes this is "the first full report of a sermon of Paul's that Luke has preserved for us... The keynotes of Paul's theology as found in his Epistles appear in this sermon." Calvin outlines its structure: Israel's election and history (preparing for the Messiah), Jesus as the promised Christ, his power and office, and a closing warning.

The thin comment matches the verse, which opens the sermon. The witnesses agree: Paul stood, beckoned for silence, and addressed two classes — "Men of Israel" (the Jews) and "ye that fear God" (the God-fearing Gentiles), tactfully showing his regard (Chrysostom). The graphic touches (the gesture, the standing) suggest an eyewitness report (Ellicott). This is the first full report of a Pauline sermon (Robertson), in which the keynotes of his theology appear. Calvin outlines its structure: Israel's election and history preparing for the Messiah, Jesus the promised Christ, his power and office, and a closing warning. The verse displays Paul's wise and conciliatory method: beginning with what his hearers valued (their history and the fear of God) before leading them to Christ. The verse marks the beginning of the great discourse that would proclaim Jesus as the fulfillment of Israel's hope. Addressing both Jews and God-fearers, Paul builds from their shared Scriptures and history toward the gospel of the risen Savior. The sermon, the first fully reported, is the model of Paul's preaching to a synagogue audience: respectful, scriptural, historical, and climaxing in Christ. The apostle to the Gentiles begins his great proclamation.
17:
"The God of this people of Israel chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they dwelt as strangers in the land of Egypt, and with an high arm brought he them out of it." The witnesses note the opening of the historical recital and the theme of God's electing love. The grammatical voices note Paul follows Stephen's method (ch. 7) of rehearsing Israel's history. Ellicott: "It was a theme which Israelites were never tired of listening to. It showed that the Apostles recognised it as the history of God's chosen people."

On "chose our fathers," the Reformed Calvin draws out the free electing love: "How came it to pass that only the children of Abraham were the Church and inheritance of God, save only because it pleased God to dissever them from other nations? For there was no worthiness to distinguish them; but the difference began at the love of God, wherewith he did freely love Abraham." The whole history flows from God's free choice.

On "with an high arm," the witnesses note the figure of God's mighty power. Barnes: "an arm lifted up, or stretched out, denotes that strength exerted to the utmost," referring to the plagues and the exodus. Wesley notes these verses (17-22) "contain the whole sum of the Old Testament."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opens his historical recital with God's electing love — "the God of this people chose our fathers" and brought them out of Egypt "with an high arm." Following Stephen's method (ch. 7), Paul rehearses the history Israel loved, recognizing it as the story of God's chosen people (Ellicott). Calvin draws out the free electing love: Israel was chosen not for any worthiness but by God's free love of Abraham, the fountain of all the benefits that followed. The "high arm" figures God's mighty power in the exodus (Barnes). The verse displays the foundation of Paul's argument: God's gracious choice of Israel, leading (as the sermon will show) to the promised Messiah. The verse marks the beginning of the historical survey that prepares for Christ: God chose, exalted, and delivered Israel — all flowing from his free electing love, and all tending toward the Savior promised to David's seed (v.23). Paul builds from the history his hearers revered, showing that the God who chose and delivered their fathers has now fulfilled his promise in Jesus. The free love of God, the root of Israel's whole history, finds its end and crown in the gift of Christ.
18:
"And about the time of forty years suffered he their manners in the wilderness." The witnesses note the forty years in the wilderness — and address a textual variant. The grammatical voices note two readings differing by one letter: "suffered their manners" (etropophoresen) or "nourished/bore them as a nurse" (etrophophoresen). Barnes and Clarke prefer the latter, fitting the conciliatory tone: "It is not probable that Paul would have commenced a discourse by reminding them of the obstinacy and wickedness of the nation... but by reminding them of the mercies of God." Ellicott and Robertson note the better MSS and the parallel in Deuteronomy 1:31 (the LXX) support "nourished."

On the meaning, if "suffered their manners," the witnesses note God's patience with a stubborn people. Calvin (reading "suffered"): "the mercifulness of God is expressed in suffering the people, whom he knew to be stubborn and disobedient... the election of God was the cause that his goodness did strive with the wickedness of the people."

The witnesses note that either reading conveys God's gracious dealing. Whether bearing with their stubbornness or nursing them like a father carrying his child (Deuteronomy 1:31), the verse displays God's patient goodness toward Israel in the wilderness. The variant, the witnesses note, turns on a single letter and either way magnifies God's mercy.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the forty years in the wilderness displayed God's gracious dealing with Israel. A textual variant turns on one letter: "suffered their manners" (bore with their stubbornness) or "nourished them" (as a nurse/father carries a child, Deuteronomy 1:31). Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson prefer "nourished," as fitting the conciliatory tone of the sermon (Paul recounting God's mercies, not Israel's faults); Calvin (reading "suffered") draws God's patience with a stubborn people, his electing love striving with their wickedness. The verse displays God's patient goodness toward Israel in the wilderness — whether bearing with them or nursing them, a testimony to his gracious care. The verse continues the historical recital of God's mercies, the variant either way magnifying his patience and love. The forty years, a time of Israel's repeated rebellion, are recounted not to upbraid but to display God's faithfulness — the God who bore with or nourished his people through the wilderness, keeping covenant despite their stubbornness. Paul builds his argument on God's persistent grace toward Israel, leading toward the promised Savior.
19:
"And when he had destroyed seven nations in the land of Chanaan, he divided their land to them by lot." The witnesses note the conquest of Canaan and the division of the land. The grammatical voices identify the seven nations: the Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites (Deuteronomy 7:1) — "the seven nations" commonly named in Jewish writings (Clarke, Gill). On "destroyed," Barnes notes it means subdued or broken up as nations, not all put to death.

On "divided their land by lot," the witnesses note the distribution recorded in Joshua. Robertson notes the better reading "gave them for an inheritance" (katekleronomesen), the land distributed by lot (Numbers 26:55-56; Joshua 14-19). The lot was used to determine the tribal portions.

The verse the witnesses read as part of the recital of God's benefits. The conquest and division of the promised land were the fulfillment of God's promise to the fathers, the crown of the deliverance from Egypt. Paul continues to display God's faithful dealing with Israel.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God destroyed (subdued) the seven nations of Canaan (Deuteronomy 7:1, the commonly named seven, Clarke) and divided their land to Israel by lot — the distribution recorded in Joshua (the better reading "gave for an inheritance," Robertson). The verse continues the recital of God's benefits: the conquest and division of the promised land, the fulfillment of his promise to the fathers and the crown of the deliverance from Egypt. The verse displays God's faithful dealing with Israel: he not only brought them out of Egypt but brought them into Canaan, giving them the land of the seven nations as their inheritance. The verse marks another stage in the historical survey leading to Christ: the gift of the land, the fulfillment of the promise, the establishment of Israel in their inheritance. Paul recounts the great acts of God — election, exodus, wilderness care, conquest — all displaying his faithfulness and all tending toward the promised Messiah of David's seed. The God who gave Israel the land would give them, in due time, the Savior promised to their fathers. The history of God's benefits builds toward its climax in Christ.
20:
"And after that he gave unto them judges about the space of four hundred and fifty years, until Samuel the prophet." The witnesses note the period of the judges — and a famous chronological crux. The difficulty: the 450 years seems to conflict with 1 Kings 6:1 (480 years from the Exodus to the temple). The witnesses offer solutions. The true text (better MSS) places the 450 years before "he gave judges," reckoning from the choice of the fathers (or Isaac's birth) to the division of Canaan (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke): "the 450 years runs from the birth of Isaac to the actual conquest of Canaan and does not cover the period of the Judges" (Robertson).

Barnes notes the chronology accords with Josephus and the common reckoning of Paul's time: "Paul would naturally use the chronology which was in current, common use, among the Jews." Ellicott notes Josephus gives 592 years from Exodus to temple, leaving about 443 for the judges, agreeing with the Received text. The witnesses note the difficulty of ancient chronology and the use of round numbers.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson of God's patience through the judges' period: "here is another testimony of the infinite goodness of God toward the Jews, in that he pardoned so many backslidings in them... So that he saved the body thereof alive, amidst many deaths, four whole ages and one-half."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God gave judges for about 450 years until Samuel — and address the chronological crux (the apparent conflict with 1 Kings 6:1's 480 years). The true text (better MSS) places the 450 years before "he gave judges," reckoning from the choice of the fathers/Isaac to the conquest, not over the judges' period (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke), removing the difficulty; or Paul used the common reckoning of his day (agreeing with Josephus, Barnes), or round numbers. Calvin draws the lesson of God's patience through the period of the judges, pardoning Israel's many backslidings. The verse displays God's continued faithfulness through the era of the judges, despite Israel's repeated rebellion. The verse continues the historical recital toward the monarchy and David: the judges, then Samuel, then Saul (v.21), then David (v.22), of whose seed the Savior came (v.23). The chronological crux, the witnesses note, does not impugn the truth of the history but reflects the difficulties of ancient chronology and Paul's use of the current reckoning. God's patient governance of Israel through the judges leads on toward the promised Messiah of David's line.
21:
"And afterward they desired a king: and God gave unto them Saul the son of Cis, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, by the space of forty years." The witnesses note the rise of the monarchy under Saul. On the people's desire for a king, the witnesses note it was a rejection of the theocracy. Robertson: "They were tired of a theocracy" (1 Samuel 8:5). It was predicted (Deuteronomy 17:14-15) yet displeasing to God (1 Samuel 8:7).

On Saul of the tribe of Benjamin, the witnesses note Paul's personal connection. Ellicott: "It is natural to think of the Apostle as dwelling on the memory of the hero-king of the tribe to which he himself belonged (Philippians 3:5). The very fact that he had so recently renounced the name would bring the associations more vividly to his recollection." Paul, of Benjamin and once named Saul, dwells on the first King Saul.

On the forty years, the witnesses note the duration (not given in the OT but confirmed by Josephus, Ellicott, Barnes); some include Samuel's judgeship in the count (Wesley, Barnes). The Reformed Calvin notes God's overruling: the people's wicked desire for a king became "a new and incredible occasion to erect the kingdom whence Christ should afterward come."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Israel desired a king (a rejection of the theocracy, 1 Samuel 8, Robertson), and God gave them Saul of Benjamin for forty years. Paul, himself of Benjamin and once named Saul (Philippians 3:5), naturally dwells on the first King Saul (Ellicott). The forty years (confirmed by Josephus, perhaps including Samuel's judgeship, Barnes, Wesley) mark the first reign. Calvin notes God's overruling: the people's wicked desire for a king became the occasion for establishing the kingdom from which Christ would come. The verse displays God's providence working even through Israel's sinful demand: the monarchy, begun in rebellion, would lead (through David) to the Messiah. The verse continues the historical recital toward David and Christ: Saul the first king, then David (v.22), of whose seed the Savior came (v.23). The verse marks the transition to the monarchy — the stage on which the promise of the Davidic Messiah would be set. God, overruling the people's misguided desire, uses the kingship to advance his purpose: the throne of David, and from David's seed, the promised Savior. The history moves toward its climax in Christ, the true and eternal King of David's line.
22:
"And when he had removed him, he raised up unto them David to be their king; to whom also he gave testimony, and said, I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after mine own heart, which shall fulfil all my will." The witnesses note the raising up of David and the great testimony to him — and explain its meaning. On "removed him," the witnesses note Saul's rejection (for his disobedience, 1 Samuel 15) and David's anointing. On the quotation, the grammatical voices note it is a composite (Psalm 89:20; 1 Samuel 13:14), in the manner of the rabbis (Ellicott).

On "a man after mine own heart," the witnesses carefully explain it of David's public, royal character, not his private sinlessness. Barnes: "This refers, doubtless, rather to the public than to the private character of David; or to his character as a king. It means that he would make the will of God the great rule and law of his reign." Clarke: "He was a man after God's own heart, because he ruled the people according to the Divine will... not as to his private, but his public character." Wesley warns against making it the character of David "in every part of his behaviour," for in the matter of Uriah he was far from it.

The Reformed Calvin adds two senses: God respected "the continual course of his life, than unto every of his particular actions," and set him forth "for his Christ's sake" — the title pointing beyond David to Christ, the true King after God's heart.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God removed Saul (for his disobedience) and raised up David, testifying "a man after mine own heart, which shall fulfil all my will" (a composite quotation, Psalm 89:20; 1 Samuel 13:14, Ellicott). The witnesses carefully explain "after mine own heart" of David's public, royal character — ruling according to God's will, unlike the disobedient Saul — not his private sinlessness (Barnes, Clarke); Wesley warns against applying it to all his behaviour (the matter of Uriah being far from it). Calvin adds that God respected the continual course of David's life and set him forth for Christ's sake, the title pointing beyond David to the true King. The verse displays the climax of the historical recital: David, the king after God's heart, of whose seed the Savior would come (v.23). The verse marks the crucial transition: from David, God raised up Jesus the Savior (v.23). "A man after God's own heart" describes David's faithful kingship and points beyond him to Christ, the perfect King who truly fulfilled all God's will. The history reaches its goal: the throne of David, and from David's seed, the promised Messiah, Jesus.
23:
"Of this man's seed hath God according to his promise raised unto Israel a Saviour, Jesus:" The witnesses note the transition from David to Christ — the climax of the sermon's first part. The grammatical voices note the emphatic "of this man's seed." Robertson: "Paul goes no further than David because he suggests to him Jesus, descendant in the flesh from David." The Savior came of David's line, according to promise.

On "according to his promise," the witnesses note the fulfillment of prophecy. Robertson cites the promises (2 Samuel 7; Psalm 132:11; Isaiah 11:1; Jeremiah 23:5). Calvin: "in sending Christ, the Lord had respect only unto his own faithfulness and goodness; for he sent him because he had promised so to do." The coming of Christ was no sudden novelty but the fulfillment of the long-promised hope.

On "a Saviour, Jesus," the witnesses note the name's meaning. Calvin: "he uttered one thing twice... because he meant to declare that Christ is indeed and doth perform that which the name given him by God, by the voice of the angel, doth import" (Jesus = Savior). Robertson notes "Israel" here means the Jewish people, later enlarged to the spiritual Israel.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reaches the climax — "of this man's seed [David's] hath God according to his promise raised unto Israel a Saviour, Jesus." The Savior came of David's line (Robertson), according to the long-standing promise (2 Samuel 7; Psalm 132:11; Isaiah 11; Jeremiah 23, Robertson) — no sudden novelty but the fulfillment of prophecy (Calvin). The name "Jesus" means Savior, declaring what he performs (Calvin). The verse displays the goal of the whole historical recital: from David's seed, according to promise, God raised up the Savior, Jesus. The verse marks the great transition from the history to the gospel: all the preceding survey (election, exodus, conquest, judges, monarchy, David) tended toward this — the promised Savior of David's line. Paul, having shown God's faithful dealing with Israel, now proclaims its fulfillment: Jesus, of David's seed, the Savior promised to the fathers. The verse is the hinge of the sermon, turning from the past history of God's mercies to the present gospel of the risen Christ. The promise made to David is fulfilled in Jesus, the Savior raised up for Israel — and, as the sermon will show, for all who believe.
24:
"When John had first preached before his coming the baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel." The witnesses note the introduction of John the Baptist as the forerunner. The grammatical voices note the phrase. Robertson: "John heralding beforehand, as a herald before the king... before the face of his entering in." John prepared the way before Christ's public coming (Malachi 3:1; Luke 1:76).

On "the baptism of repentance," the witnesses note John's message and ministry. Robertson: "Baptism marked by, characterized by repentance... The very phrase used of John's preaching in Mark 1:4; Luke 3:3." Calvin notes the novelty of John's baptism as "a token of great alteration," preparing the people for Christ.

On John's role, the Reformed Calvin notes why Paul cites him: "Paul bringeth in his testimony, that he may prove to the Jews that he preached no false Christ, but the true Christ of God, whom that most famous forerunner had before commended." John, esteemed a prophet by all, was a powerful witness to Christ.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: John the Baptist, the forerunner, preached the baptism of repentance before Christ's public coming — heralding the king (Robertson, Malachi 3:1), his baptism a token of the great alteration at hand (Calvin). Paul cites John, Calvin notes, to prove he preached the true Christ whom the famous forerunner had commended — John, esteemed a prophet by all, being a powerful witness. The verse displays the divinely-appointed witness to Christ: John, preparing the way, preaching repentance, and pointing to the Savior. The verse continues the gospel proclamation: having declared Jesus the promised Savior (v.23), Paul adduces John's testimony as confirmation. The forerunner's ministry — the baptism of repentance, the preparation of the people — was the prelude to Christ, and his witness (vv.25) the divine attestation to Jesus. The verse marks the transition from the promise (David's seed, v.23) to the witness (John the Baptist), establishing Jesus as the true Christ on the testimony of the prophet whom all Israel revered. John prepared the way; Jesus is the one who came after, whose shoes John was not worthy to loose (v.25).
25:
"And as John fulfilled his course, he said, Whom think ye that I am? I am not he. But, behold, there cometh one after me, whose shoes of his feet I am not worthy to loose." The witnesses note John's self-effacing testimony to Christ. On "fulfilled his course," the grammatical voices note the figure of John's ministry as a race (Robertson; cf. Paul's own course, Acts 20:24; 2 Timothy 4:7). John, completing his ministry, bore witness to the one greater than himself.

On John's disclaimer, the witnesses note his humility. Chrysostom (under v.16): "when men were bringing the glory to him, he declines it... to reject it when all men are ready to give it, and not only to reject it, but to do so with such humility." Calvin: "his testimony doth deserve greater credit, in that he doth willingly refuse the honor offered him."

On "not worthy to loose his shoes," the witnesses note the proverbial expression of utmost lowliness. Calvin: "he abaseth himself so much as he can, lest his greatness darken Christ's glory... how great soever he be, yet he is nothing in respect of Christ." Robertson marvels at Paul's accurate knowledge of John's words (Mark 1:7; Luke 3:16).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: John, fulfilling his course (his ministry a race, Robertson), bore self-effacing witness to Christ — "I am not he... there cometh one after me, whose shoes I am not worthy to loose." His humility, refusing the honor men offered and pointing to the greater one (Chrysostom, Calvin), made his testimony all the more credible. The "not worthy to loose his shoes" is the proverbial figure of utmost lowliness, John abasing himself lest his greatness darken Christ's glory (Calvin). The verse displays the model witness: the forerunner who, though great, declined all honor and pointed wholly to Christ. The verse marks the climax of John's testimony: the Baptist, esteemed by all, disclaiming the Messiahship and exalting the one who came after — the strongest possible witness to Jesus as the Christ. John's humility is the pattern of all faithful witness: to decrease that Christ may increase, to point men away from oneself to the Savior. Paul adduces this testimony to establish Jesus as the true Christ, attested by the forerunner whom all Israel revered. The greatest of the prophets confessed himself unworthy to loose the shoes of the one he heralded — Jesus, the promised Savior.
26:
"Men and brethren, children of the stock of Abraham, and whosoever among you feareth God, to you is the word of this salvation sent." The witnesses note Paul's direct, earnest application to his hearers. The grammatical voices note the renewed address to the two classes. Ellicott: "The two classes are, as before (Acts 13:16), again pointedly contrasted with each other" — the Jews ("children of the stock of Abraham") and the God-fearing Gentiles ("whosoever feareth God").

On "to you is the word of this salvation sent," the witnesses note the personal offer. Barnes: "He means here to address particularly the native Jews... The Saviour was sent to that nation, and the design was to offer to them first the message of life." The "word of this salvation" is the gospel of the Savior Jesus (v.23).

On "this salvation," Ellicott notes the demonstrative: "the salvation which St. Paul proclaimed rested on the work of Jesus the Saviour (Acts 13:23), and was found in union with Him." The salvation offered is specifically that wrought by Jesus.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul makes his direct, earnest application — addressing again the two classes, Jews ("children of the stock of Abraham") and God-fearing Gentiles ("whosoever feareth God"), and declaring "to you is the word of this salvation sent." The "word of this salvation" is the gospel of the Savior Jesus (v.23), offered first to the Jews (Barnes), the salvation resting on the work of Jesus (Ellicott). The verse displays the personal offer of the gospel: having proclaimed Jesus the promised Savior (v.23) and adduced John's witness (vv.24-25), Paul now presses the message home — this salvation is sent to you. The verse marks the turn from proclamation to application: Paul addresses his hearers directly, offering them the salvation of Christ. The earnest "to you is the word of this salvation sent" is the heart of the gospel appeal: the message of Jesus the Savior, sent to Jews and God-fearers alike, calling them to receive it. The verse, closing this block, sets up the climax of the sermon (vv.27-41), where Paul would declare the death, resurrection, and saving work of Jesus, and the remission of sins through his name. The word of salvation is sent; the hearers are called to believe.
27:
"For they that dwell at Jerusalem, and their rulers, because they knew him not, nor yet the voices of the prophets which are read every sabbath day, they have fulfilled them in condemning him." The witnesses note that the Jews, in ignorance, fulfilled the prophecies by condemning Christ. Gill notes Paul speaks not of all (for some knew Jesus was the Messiah yet rejected him), but of those "really ignorant of Christ"; choosing not to provoke, but to plead their ignorance. The witnesses note the dreadful irony: the Jerusalem Jews and their rulers, not knowing Christ nor understanding the prophets they read every sabbath, fulfilled those very prophecies by condemning him. The witnesses note the ignorance and the fulfilment. The witnesses note prophecy fulfilled in condemning Christ.

The witnesses note the irony: the Jews, ignorant of Christ and the prophets, fulfilled the prophecies by condemning him.

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilment of prophecy in the condemnation of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the dreadful irony that the Jews, ignorant of Christ and of the prophets they read, fulfilled those prophecies by condemning him. The witnesses note the ignorance: "because they knew him not, nor yet the voices of the prophets which are read every sabbath day" — the Jerusalem Jews and their rulers did not know Christ as the Messiah, nor understand the prophets which were read in the synagogues every sabbath; their ignorance of both the person and the prophecy. The expositors observe Gill's careful note: Paul speaks not of all the Jews (for some knew Jesus was the Messiah yet rejected him, sinning against light), but of those really ignorant of Christ; pleading their ignorance, choosing not to provoke but to mitigate; the apostle's tenderness in laying their sin to ignorance. The witnesses press the dreadful irony: "they have fulfilled them in condemning him" — the very prophecies they read every sabbath, but did not understand, they fulfilled by condemning Christ; the prophets foretold the rejection and death of the Messiah, and the Jews, in condemning him, accomplished what was written; their ignorance the instrument of the fulfilment. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence overruling sin: the Jews' condemnation of Christ, sinful as it was, fulfilled the Scriptures; God overruling their ignorance and malice to accomplish the foretold death of the Messiah (Acts 2:23); the prophecy fulfilled through the very ones who rejected it. The witnesses observe the solemnity of fulfilling Scripture unawares: the Jews read the prophets weekly, yet, not understanding, fulfilled them in their sin; a warning that men may handle the Scriptures and yet be ignorant of their meaning, even fulfilling them against themselves. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the irony of the Jews fulfilling the prophecies they read, the providence overruling their ignorance, and the warning against handling Scripture without understanding. The verse thus records the fulfilment of prophecy in the condemnation of Christ — the ignorant Jews accomplishing what the prophets foretold — teaching the providence that overruled their sin and the warning of handling Scripture without understanding it.
28:
"And though they found no cause of death in him, yet desired they Pilate that he should be slain." The witnesses note the innocence of Christ and the injustice of his condemnation. Barnes presses the proof: "they found no crime which deserved death; this is conclusively shown by the trial itself; after all their efforts, after the treason of Judas, after their employing false witnesses, still no crime was laid to his charge"; the Sanhedrim, unable to substantiate blasphemy before Pilate, sought his death on the ground of sedition. The witnesses note that the Jews, though finding no cause of death in the innocent Christ, yet pressed Pilate to have him slain — the injustice of his condemnation, the guiltless put to death. The witnesses note the innocence of Christ. The witnesses note the unjust condemnation.

The witnesses note Christ's innocence — no cause of death found — yet the Jews demanded his death of Pilate.

So the witnesses make the verse the innocence of Christ and the injustice of his death. The lesson the expositors draw is the established innocence of Christ, condemned not by justice but by the malice of his enemies. The witnesses note the innocence: "though they found no cause of death in him" — the Jews, with all their efforts, found no crime in Christ deserving death; Barnes presses the proof — after the treason of Judas, the employing of false witnesses, all their attempts, no crime could be laid to his charge; the trial itself conclusively showing his innocence. The expositors observe the failure of the charges: the Sanhedrim condemned him for blasphemy, yet knew they could not substantiate this before Pilate, and so sought his death on the ground of sedition; the charges shifting, none proven; the innocent unable to be convicted of any crime. The witnesses press the injustice: "yet desired they Pilate that he should be slain" — finding no cause of death, the Jews yet demanded that Pilate put him to death; the guiltless condemned not by justice but by the will of his enemies; the death of the innocent procured by malice. The expositors draw the lesson on the established innocence of Christ: his innocence was proved by the very trial — no crime found, the charges unproven; Christ the spotless Lamb, condemned though guiltless (1 Peter 2:22, "who did no sin"); the injustice of his death the measure of his enemies' malice and the proof of his sinlessness. The witnesses observe the use Paul makes of this: he shows the Jews that Christ was condemned unjustly, the innocent put to death; the injustice of the condemnation an argument that the death was not deserved but the malice of men, fulfilling prophecy and accomplishing redemption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the innocence of Christ, established by his trial, and the injustice of his condemnation, the guiltless put to death by malice. The verse thus records the innocence of Christ and the injustice of his death — no cause found, yet death demanded — teaching the established innocence of the spotless Lamb, condemned by malice, not justice.
29:
"And when they had fulfilled all that was written of him, they took him down from the tree, and laid him in a sepulchre." The witnesses note the fulfilment of all the Scriptures in Christ's death and burial. Gill details what was fulfilled: the reproaches, the buffeting and scourging, the piercing of his hands and feet, the crucifixion between two thieves, the parting of his raiment, the piercing of his side — all written in the Psalms and Prophets. Barnes notes the taking down and burial were done by the Jews (Joseph of Arimathea, Nicodemus). The witnesses note that, having fulfilled all that was written, Christ was taken down from the cross and laid in a tomb — the Scriptures fulfilled to the last detail. The witnesses note all the Scriptures fulfilled. The witnesses note the burial.

The witnesses note all the Scriptures fulfilled in Christ's death — then taken down and laid in a tomb.

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilment of all the Scriptures in Christ's death and burial. The lesson the expositors draw is the exact and complete fulfilment of prophecy in the sufferings, death, and burial of Christ. The witnesses note the comprehensive fulfilment: "when they had fulfilled all that was written of him" — all that the prophets had foretold of the Messiah's sufferings was fulfilled; Gill details the particulars — the reproaches, the buffeting and scourging, the piercing of his hands and feet (Psalm 22:16), the crucifixion between two thieves (Isaiah 53:12), the parting of his raiment (Psalm 22:18), the piercing of his side (Zechariah 12:10) — all written in the Psalms and Prophets, all accomplished. The expositors observe the exactness of the fulfilment: every detail of the Messiah's sufferings, foretold in Scripture, was fulfilled in Christ; the prophecies accomplished to the letter; the death of Christ the precise fulfilment of the prophetic word. The witnesses press the taking down and burial: "they took him down from the tree, and laid him in a sepulchre" — Barnes notes this was done by the Jews (Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus, Jews who buried him); the burial itself fulfilling prophecy (Isaiah 53:9, "with the rich in his death"). The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of his death: the taking down and burial established that Christ was truly dead — taken from the cross, laid in the tomb; the reality of his death confirmed, the ground laid for the resurrection that follows (v.30). The witnesses observe the providence in the fulfilment: the Jews and Romans, in their malice, fulfilled the Scriptures to the last detail; God overruling their deeds to accomplish the foretold death and burial of the Messiah; the prophecy fulfilled through the very ones who opposed it. The witnesses draw the lesson on the harmony of prophecy and event: the death and burial of Christ fulfilled all that was written; the Scripture and the event in perfect agreement; the proof that Jesus is the Messiah of whom the prophets spoke. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the exact fulfilment of all the Scriptures in Christ's death and burial, the certainty of his death, and the harmony of prophecy and event. The verse thus records the fulfilment of all the Scriptures and the burial — taken down, laid in a tomb — teaching the exact and complete fulfilment of prophecy in the death and burial of Christ.
30:
"But God raised him from the dead:" The witnesses note the great turn — the resurrection of Christ by God. Clarke presses the proof it gave: "But God raised him from the dead — and thus gave the fullest proof of his innocence; God alone can raise the dead; and he would not work a miracle so very extraordinary, but on some extraordinary occasion." Gill notes that though his life was taken by man, and he was certainly dead and buried, "he could not be held with the cords of death, but God the Father raised him from the dead by his power." The witnesses note the great contrast: man killed Christ, but God raised him — the resurrection vindicating his innocence and crowning the gospel. The witnesses note the resurrection. The witnesses note the great turn.

The witnesses note the great turn — man killed Christ, but God raised him, vindicating his innocence.

So the witnesses make the verse the resurrection of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the great contrast between man's killing and God's raising, and the vindication of Christ's innocence in the resurrection. The witnesses note the turn: "But God raised him from the dead" — the great "but" marking the contrast; man killed Christ (v.28-29), but God raised him; the divine reversal of the human crime; the resurrection the answer to the cross. The expositors observe Gill's note on the power of God: though Christ's life was taken by man, and he was certainly dead and buried, he could not be held by the cords of death (Acts 2:24), but God the Father raised him by his power; the resurrection the act of God's almighty power, undoing what death had done. The witnesses press Clarke's point on the proof it gave: the resurrection gave the fullest proof of Christ's innocence; God alone can raise the dead, and would not work so extraordinary a miracle but on an extraordinary occasion; by raising Christ, God vindicated him — declared him innocent, owned him as his Son, reversed the verdict of men. The expositors draw the lesson on the vindication of Christ: man condemned and killed him as guilty; God raised him, declaring him righteous; the resurrection God's reversal of the unjust condemnation, his seal on the innocence of his Son; "declared to be the Son of God with power, by the resurrection from the dead" (Romans 1:4). The witnesses observe the central place of the resurrection in the gospel: the resurrection the great fact Paul preaches, the foundation of the gospel; Christ killed but raised, the death and the rising together the heart of the message; the resurrection crowning the work of redemption. The expositors note the contrast Paul draws between human malice and divine power: the Jews and Romans did their worst, killing and burying Christ; but God raised him, overruling their crime, conquering death; the divine power triumphing over the human malice. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the great turn of the resurrection, the vindication of Christ's innocence, and the divine power that raised him. The verse thus records the resurrection of Christ — man killed him, but God raised him — teaching the great contrast of human malice and divine power and the vindication of Christ's innocence in the resurrection.
31:
"And he was seen many days of them which came up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are his witnesses unto the people." The witnesses note the eyewitnesses of the resurrection. Barnes notes he was seen "many days" (forty days, Acts 1:3) by the apostles particularly, who were "chosen for this object, to bear witness to him." Clarke presses the recency and the living witnesses: "the thing was done but a very short time since; and many of the witnesses are still alive, and ready to attest the fact." Ellicott marks Paul's language as that of one who had conversed with the witnesses and convinced himself of their testimony. The witnesses note the risen Christ seen many days by the chosen witnesses — the resurrection attested by living eyewitnesses. The witnesses note the eyewitnesses. The witnesses note the attested resurrection.

The witnesses note the risen Christ seen many days by chosen eyewitnesses — the resurrection attested by living witnesses.

So the witnesses make the verse the eyewitnesses of the resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is the attestation of the resurrection by chosen, living eyewitnesses. The witnesses note the appearances: "he was seen many days of them which came up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem" — the risen Christ was seen, over many days (forty days, Acts 1:3), by those who had accompanied him from Galilee — the apostles and other disciples; the resurrection not a single doubtful sighting, but many appearances over a period, to many witnesses. The expositors observe the witnesses' qualification: "who are his witnesses unto the people" — Barnes notes the apostles were chosen for this very object, to bear witness to the resurrection; the appointed, qualified witnesses, who had seen the risen Lord and were to testify of him. The witnesses press Clarke's point on the recency and the living witnesses: the resurrection had happened but a short time since, and many of the witnesses were still alive, ready to attest the fact; the testimony of living eyewitnesses, available to be questioned; the resurrection not a distant legend but a recent, attested fact. The expositors draw the lesson on the foundation of the gospel in eyewitness testimony: the resurrection rests on the testimony of those who saw the risen Christ over many days; credible witnesses, chosen and qualified, testifying to the people; the gospel built on attested fact, not invention. The witnesses observe Ellicott's note on Paul's language: it is the language of one who had conversed with the witnesses and convinced himself of the truth of their testimony; Paul, though not himself an eyewitness of the forty days, had verified the testimony; the resurrection confirmed to him by the witnesses. The expositors note the use Paul makes of this: he appeals to the eyewitnesses as proof of the resurrection; the testimony of those who saw the risen Christ the ground of his preaching; the fact established by witnesses. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the eyewitnesses of the resurrection, the attestation by living witnesses, and the foundation of the gospel in their testimony. The verse thus records the eyewitnesses of the resurrection — Christ seen many days by his chosen witnesses — teaching the attestation of the resurrection by chosen, living eyewitnesses and the foundation of the gospel in their testimony.
32:
"And we declare unto you glad tidings, how that the promise which was made unto the fathers," The witnesses note the gospel as the fulfilment of the promise to the fathers. Gill describes the glad tidings: "the whole of the Gospel, concerning the incarnation, obedience, sufferings, death, and resurrection of Christ, and the benefits arising from thence, as peace, pardon, righteousness, life, and salvation."
