Barnes notes the promise refers to all that was spoken in the Old Testament of the Messiah; to a Jew nothing could be more grateful than that the Messiah had come. The witnesses note Paul declaring the gospel as glad tidings — the fulfilment of the promise made to the fathers, the coming of the Messiah long promised. The witnesses note the glad tidings. The witnesses note the promise to the fathers.

The witnesses note the gospel declared as glad tidings — the fulfilment of the promise made to the fathers.

So the witnesses make the verse the gospel as the fulfilment of the promise to the fathers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel is glad tidings, the fulfilment of the ancient promise of the Messiah. The witnesses note the proclamation: "we declare unto you glad tidings" — Paul and Barnabas declare the gospel as glad tidings, good news; Gill describes its content — the whole gospel, concerning the incarnation, obedience, sufferings, death, and resurrection of Christ, and the benefits of peace, pardon, righteousness, life, and salvation; the message of salvation good news to sinners. The expositors observe the substance of the good news: "the promise which was made unto the fathers" — the gospel is the fulfilment of the promise God made to the fathers; the coming of the Messiah, long promised throughout the Old Testament, now accomplished; the glad tidings that the promise is fulfilled. The witnesses press what the promise was, which Barnes marks: the promise refers to all that was spoken in the Old Testament of the Messiah — the promises to Abraham, to David, in the prophets; the whole hope of Israel; and to a Jew, nothing could be more grateful than to hear that the Messiah had come, the promise fulfilled. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel as the fulfilment of the promise: the gospel is no new thing, but the fulfilment of the ancient promise; the Messiah promised to the fathers now come; the continuity of the gospel with the Old Testament hope; the promise and the fulfilment one. The witnesses observe the gladness of the tidings: the gospel is "glad tidings" — good news, joyful, to be received with gladness; the fulfilment of the promise the cause of joy; the coming of the Saviour the best of news to the Jew and the sinner. The expositors note Paul's appeal to the promise: he declares the gospel as the fulfilment of the promise the Jews cherished — meeting them on the ground of their own hope, showing the Messiah they awaited had come; the gospel the answer to their expectation. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gospel as good news: the believer marks the gospel as glad tidings, the fulfilment of the promise to the fathers, and the joy of the Messiah come. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the glad tidings, the fulfilment of the ancient promise, and the gospel as the joy of the promise fulfilled. The verse thus records the gospel declared as glad tidings — the fulfilment of the promise to the fathers — teaching that the gospel is good news, the fulfilment of the ancient promise of the Messiah.
33:
"God hath fulfilled the same unto us their children, in that he hath raised up Jesus again; as it is also written in the second psalm, Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee." The witnesses note the fulfilment of the promise in raising up Jesus, and the meaning of "begotten." Lightfoot discusses the second psalm (Psalm 2:7). The witnesses note the debate on "raised up Jesus again": whether it means God's raising him up into the world (sending, incarnating the Messiah, as the prophet "raised up," Acts 3:22), or his raising him from the dead (the resurrection); and "this day have I begotten thee" — the declaration of his sonship, applied (with Romans 1:4) to the resurrection, or to his eternal generation. The witnesses note the promise fulfilled in Jesus. The witnesses note the begotten Son.

The witnesses note the promise fulfilled in raising up Jesus — "this day have I begotten thee" (Psalm 2:7).

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilment of the promise in raising up Jesus and the meaning of "begotten." The lesson the expositors draw is that God fulfilled the promise in Jesus, declared his Son in the words of the second psalm. The witnesses note the fulfilment: "God hath fulfilled the same unto us their children, in that he hath raised up Jesus again" — God fulfilled the promise made to the fathers, to their children (Paul's generation), by raising up Jesus; the promise accomplished in the sending and rising of Christ. The expositors weigh the meaning of "raised up Jesus again." Some take it of God's raising him up into the world — sending, manifesting the Messiah (as "raised up a prophet," Acts 3:22; "raised up... a Saviour," Acts 13:23); the begetting then the sending. Others take it of the resurrection — God raising him from the dead (the theme of the surrounding verses); the begetting then connected with the resurrection. The witnesses observe that the citation, "Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee" (Psalm 2:7), is the declaration of Christ's sonship; and the application is variously made — to his eternal generation (the Son begotten of the Father from eternity); to his incarnation (the Son begotten in time); or, with Paul's own use in Romans 1:4 ("declared to be the Son of God... by the resurrection"), to the resurrection as the public declaration and "begetting" of the Son, his being brought forth from the grave as the firstborn from the dead. The expositors note that the resurrection-reading fits the context best: Paul is proving the resurrection, and "this day have I begotten thee" he applies to the day of the resurrection, when Christ was declared the Son of God with power; the begetting the bringing forth from death. The witnesses draw the lesson on the sonship of Christ: the second psalm declares Jesus the Son of God; God owning him as his Son, in the sending, the begetting, and above all the resurrection; the divine sonship of Christ attested by the Scripture. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the promise fulfilled in Jesus, the declaration of his sonship in the second psalm, and the resurrection as the begetting of the Son. The verse thus records the promise fulfilled in raising up Jesus — Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee — teaching the fulfilment of the promise in Christ and his declaration as the Son of God.
34:
"And as concerning that he raised him up from the dead, now no more to return to corruption, he said on this wise, I will give you the sure mercies of David." The witnesses note the resurrection to incorruption and the sure mercies of David. Lightfoot notes "the sure mercies of David" (Isaiah 55:3), and that "by David was understood the Messiah," as the Rabbins themselves (Kimchi, Ibn Ezra) acknowledged. Gill notes this is a distinct article — the resurrection to die no more, supported by Scripture. The witnesses note Paul proving, from Isaiah 55:3, that Christ was raised to incorruption, no more to die — the sure mercies of David fulfilled in the risen, ever-living Christ. The witnesses note the resurrection to incorruption. The witnesses note the sure mercies of David.

The witnesses note Christ raised to incorruption, no more to die — the sure mercies of David fulfilled in him.

So the witnesses make the verse the resurrection to incorruption. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ was raised to die no more, fulfilling the promise of the sure mercies of David. The witnesses note the great point: "he raised him up from the dead, now no more to return to corruption" — Christ was raised not to die again (as Lazarus and others, who rose to die once more), but to incorruption, to die no more; the resurrection to an endless, deathless life; "Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over him" (Romans 6:9). The expositors observe Gill's note that this is a distinct article from the bare raising (v.33) — the resurrection to die no more, the eternal life of the risen Christ; supported by the Scripture Paul cites. The witnesses press the citation: "I will give you the sure mercies of David" (Isaiah 55:3) — the sure, faithful, covenant mercies promised to David; Lightfoot notes that by "David" the Messiah was understood, as the Rabbins themselves (Kimchi, Ibn Ezra) acknowledged; the mercies of David fulfilled in Christ, the son of David, the Messiah. The expositors draw the connection Paul makes: the "sure mercies of David" are sure, faithful, abiding — and could not be sure if the Messiah died and remained dead; the surety of the mercies requires the ever-living Christ; the risen, deathless Christ the ground of the sure mercies; the eternal life of the Messiah securing the covenant blessings. The witnesses observe the meaning of "sure mercies": the covenant mercies promised in Christ are sure, certain, faithful — secured by the risen, ever-living Saviour; the believer's salvation resting on the abiding life of Christ, who ever lives to apply the mercies; the surety of the blessings in the deathless Christ. The expositors note the proof of the resurrection to incorruption: Paul argues from Isaiah 55:3 that the Messiah must be raised to die no more — for the mercies are sure, abiding; the deathless life of Christ proved from the surety of the promised mercies. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the resurrection of Christ to incorruption, the sure mercies of David fulfilled in the ever-living Christ, and the surety of the covenant blessings in him. The verse thus records the resurrection to incorruption — raised to die no more, the sure mercies of David — teaching that Christ lives for ever and the covenant mercies are sure in the deathless Saviour.
35:
"Wherefore he saith also in another psalm, Thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption." The witnesses note the proof from Psalm 16 that the Holy One would not see corruption. Barnes notes the citation is from Psalm 16:10, to the same intent — to prove that the Messiah must rise and live for ever; "God says by David, or David declares the promises made by God." Ellicott refers to the fuller treatment of this prophecy in Peter's Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:25-31). The witnesses note Paul citing Psalm 16:10 — the promise that God's Holy One would not see corruption — as further proof of the resurrection; the same Scripture Peter had used at Pentecost. The witnesses note the proof from Psalm 16. The witnesses note the Holy One not seeing corruption.

The witnesses note the proof from Psalm 16 — God's Holy One would not see corruption — confirming the resurrection.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof from Psalm 16. The lesson the expositors draw is the confirmation of the resurrection from the promise that the Holy One would not see corruption. The witnesses note the citation: "Thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption" — Paul cites Psalm 16:10, the promise that God would not allow his Holy One to see corruption; the same Scripture Peter had used at Pentecost (Acts 2:27); the prophecy of the resurrection of the Messiah without corruption. The expositors observe the intent of the citation, which Barnes marks: to the same end as the foregoing — to prove that the Messiah must rise and live for ever; God declaring, by David, that his Holy One would not see corruption; the promise of the resurrection. The witnesses press the meaning: "thine Holy One" — the Messiah, the Holy One of God; "shall not see corruption" — his body would not undergo the corruption of the grave, but be raised before it decayed; the resurrection on the third day, before corruption set in; the Holy One preserved from the corruption of death. The expositors draw the lesson on the prophecy of the incorruptible resurrection: the Scripture foretold that the Messiah's body would not see corruption — would be raised whole, uncorrupted; the prophecy fulfilled in Christ, raised on the third day, his body uncorrupted; the resurrection without corruption foretold and fulfilled. The witnesses observe Ellicott's reference to Peter's fuller treatment (Acts 2:25-31): the same prophecy was expounded by Peter at Pentecost, who showed it could not refer to David (whose body saw corruption) but to the Messiah; Paul here uses the same proof, which he develops in the verses following (v.36-37). The expositors note the agreement of the apostles' preaching: Peter and Paul alike cite Psalm 16 as proof of the resurrection; the consistency of the apostolic testimony; the same Scripture, the same argument, the resurrection of the incorruptible Messiah. The witnesses draw the lesson on the resurrection without corruption: the Holy One did not see corruption, but was raised whole; the prophecy fulfilled, the resurrection proved from Scripture. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the proof from Psalm 16, the Holy One not seeing corruption, and the resurrection confirmed by the prophetic word. The verse thus records the proof from Psalm 16 — the Holy One would not see corruption — teaching the confirmation of the resurrection from the prophecy of the incorruptible Messiah.
36:
"For David, after he had served his own generation by the will of God, fell on sleep, and was laid unto his fathers, and saw corruption:" The witnesses note the argument that Psalm 16 cannot refer to David. Barnes explains the design: "this verse is designed to show that the passage in Psalm 16 could not refer to David, and must therefore relate to some other person"; for David served his generation, died, was buried, and saw corruption — so the prophecy of one not seeing corruption could not be of him. The witnesses note that David, having served God's purpose in his generation, died and saw corruption — proving that the prophecy of incorruption referred not to him but to Christ. The witnesses note David seeing corruption. The witnesses note the argument from David.

The witnesses note David served his generation, died, and saw corruption — so the prophecy was not of him.

So the witnesses make the verse the argument that Psalm 16 cannot refer to David. The lesson the expositors draw is that David died and saw corruption, so the prophecy of incorruption must refer to Christ. The witnesses note David's life and death: "David, after he had served his own generation by the will of God, fell on sleep, and was laid unto his fathers, and saw corruption" — David served God's purpose in his own generation (ruling, leading, advancing the worship of God), then died, was buried with his fathers, and his body saw corruption. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the design of the verse: it is to show that the prophecy of Psalm 16 (the Holy One not seeing corruption) could not refer to David himself — for David died and saw corruption; and must therefore relate to some other, the Messiah. The witnesses press the force of the argument: David, the writer of the psalm, did see corruption; so the words "thou shalt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption" could not be fulfilled in him; they must refer to one who did not see corruption — namely, Christ, raised before corruption (v.37). The expositors draw the lesson on the application of the prophecy: the prophecy, not fulfilled in David, is fulfilled in Christ; the Scripture pointing beyond David to the Messiah; the argument (as Peter's, Acts 2:29-31) establishing that the psalm speaks of Christ, not its human author. The witnesses observe the beautiful phrase "served his own generation by the will of God": David served the purpose of God in his own time — a noble epitaph; the believer's calling to serve God's purpose in his generation, then to rest; David's life summed up as serving God's will in his day. The expositors note the contrast between David and Christ: David served his generation and died, seeing corruption; Christ, raised, saw no corruption and lives for ever; the mortal servant and the immortal Lord; the one passing, the other abiding. The witnesses draw the lesson on serving one's generation: the believer, like David, is to serve God's purpose in his own generation, then fall asleep; the life well spent that serves God's will in its day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks David's seeing corruption, the prophecy referred to Christ, and the calling to serve one's generation by the will of God. The verse thus records the argument that Psalm 16 cannot refer to David — who served his generation and saw corruption — teaching that the prophecy of incorruption refers to Christ, and the calling to serve God's purpose in one's generation.
37:
"But he, whom God raised again, saw no corruption." The witnesses note the conclusion — Christ saw no corruption. Barnes draws the conclusion: "the Lord Jesus... was raised without undergoing the usual change that succeeds death; as David had returned to corruption, and the Lord Jesus had not, it followed that this passage in Psalm 16 referred to the Messiah." Gill identifies the subject as the Lord Jesus Christ, raised by God the Father. The witnesses note the decisive conclusion: Christ, raised by God, saw no corruption — fulfilling the prophecy David could not, and proving that Psalm 16 speaks of the Messiah. The witnesses note Christ seeing no corruption. The witnesses note the conclusion.

The witnesses note the conclusion — Christ, raised, saw no corruption, fulfilling the prophecy David could not.

So the witnesses make the verse the conclusion of the argument. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, raised without corruption, fulfilled the prophecy and is therefore the Messiah of whom the psalm speaks. The witnesses note the decisive statement: "he, whom God raised again, saw no corruption" — Christ, raised by God, did not see corruption; his body, raised on the third day, did not undergo the decay of the grave; the prophecy of Psalm 16 fulfilled in him. The expositors observe Barnes's drawing of the conclusion: the Lord Jesus was raised without undergoing the usual change that succeeds death; and since David returned to corruption while Christ did not, it follows that the passage in Psalm 16 referred to the Messiah, not to David; the argument complete, the prophecy fixed on Christ. The witnesses press the contrast with David (v.36): David saw corruption; Christ saw no corruption; the prophecy of incorruption, not true of David, true of Christ; the difference decisive — the one who did not see corruption is the Holy One of the psalm. The expositors draw the lesson on the proof of Christ's Messiahship: the resurrection without corruption proves Jesus to be the Messiah of whom the psalm spoke; the prophecy, requiring one who did not see corruption, fulfilled only in Christ; the argument establishing his Messiahship from the Scripture. The witnesses observe the brevity and force of the verse: a single sentence draws the conclusion — Christ saw no corruption; the proof complete, the prophecy applied, the Messiahship established; the concise climax of the argument. The expositors note the foundation laid for the application that follows (v.38): having proved the resurrection and the Messiahship of Christ, Paul now proceeds to the benefits — forgiveness and justification; the doctrine grounded, the application to follow. The witnesses draw the lesson on the incorruptible resurrection of Christ: Christ raised whole, uncorrupted, the firstfruits of the resurrection; the prophecy fulfilled, the Messiah proved; the resurrection the seal of his person and work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ seeing no corruption, the prophecy fulfilled in him, and his Messiahship proved by the resurrection. The verse thus records the conclusion — Christ, raised, saw no corruption — teaching that he fulfilled the prophecy David could not and is therefore the Messiah of whom the psalm speaks.
38:
"Be it known unto you therefore, men and brethren, that through this man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins:" The witnesses note the great benefit — forgiveness of sins through Christ. Gill notes Paul, having discoursed of Christ's incarnation, death, and resurrection, now proceeds to the benefits arising from them, "published and made known in the everlasting Gospel" — forgiveness of sins through his blood, and justification by his righteousness (the latter in v.39). Barnes notes Paul, having proved the resurrection and Messiahship, now states the benefits of his death. The witnesses note Paul preaching forgiveness of sins through Christ — the great benefit of the gospel, the pardon proclaimed in his name. The witnesses note forgiveness of sins. The witnesses note the benefit of Christ.

The witnesses note forgiveness of sins preached through Christ — the great benefit of the gospel, the pardon proclaimed.

So the witnesses make the verse the preaching of forgiveness through Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that forgiveness of sins is the great benefit of the gospel, preached through and procured by Christ. The witnesses note the solemn announcement: "Be it known unto you therefore, men and brethren, that through this man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins" — Paul, having proved the resurrection and Messiahship of Christ, now proclaims the great benefit: forgiveness of sins, preached through Christ; the gospel's chief blessing announced. The expositors observe Gill's note on the progression: Paul, having discoursed of Christ's incarnation, death, and resurrection, now proceeds to the benefits arising from them — forgiveness of sins (this verse) and justification (v.39); the doctrine of Christ's person and work issuing in the benefits of grace. The witnesses press the means of forgiveness: "through this man" — through Christ; forgiveness of sins is preached and procured through him; not through the law, nor through man's works, but through Christ — his death the ground, his name the channel; "through his blood... the forgiveness of sins" (Ephesians 1:7). The expositors draw the lesson on forgiveness as the great benefit: the chief blessing of the gospel is the forgiveness of sins — the pardon of the guilty, the cancelling of the debt, the removal of condemnation; the good news to the sinner; the benefit Christ's death procured and the gospel proclaims. The witnesses observe the universality of the offer: "preached unto you" — forgiveness preached to the hearers, offered to all; the pardon proclaimed, held out to sinners; the gospel offer of forgiveness through Christ; "repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name" (Luke 24:47). The expositors note the connection with the foregoing: "therefore" — because Christ is risen and is the Messiah, therefore forgiveness is preached through him; the resurrection and Messiahship the ground of the offer of forgiveness; the benefit flowing from the proven person and work of Christ. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gospel of forgiveness: the believer marks forgiveness of sins as the great benefit, preached through Christ, procured by his death; the pardon proclaimed to sinners. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the forgiveness preached through Christ, the great benefit of the gospel, and the pardon offered to all. The verse thus records the preaching of forgiveness through Christ — be it known, through this man is forgiveness preached — teaching that forgiveness of sins is the great benefit of the gospel, procured by Christ and proclaimed to all.
39:
"And by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses." The witnesses note the great doctrine of justification by faith in Christ, not by the law. Gill expounds it fully: Christ's "righteousness imputed to them is the matter of their justification... and not the works of the law"; justification by Christ is "complete and perfect; it is from all sin... from all things the law can charge with, as breaches of it; from all things which the justice of God can demand satisfaction for." The witnesses note the great Pauline doctrine: all that believe are justified by Christ from all things, from which the law of Moses could not justify — justification by faith in Christ, not by the works of the law. The witnesses note justification by faith. The witnesses note the law cannot justify.

The witnesses note the great doctrine: all believers justified by Christ from all things — which the law could not do.

So the witnesses make the verse the doctrine of justification by faith in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers are justified by Christ from all things, completely and perfectly, which the law of Moses could never do. The witnesses note the great statement: "by him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses" — all who believe in Christ are justified by him; and justified from all things, from all sin and guilt, which the law could not do; the great Pauline doctrine of justification by faith, not by the works of the law. The expositors press Gill's full exposition: Christ's righteousness imputed to the believer is the matter of his justification — not the works of the law, nor obedience, nor even the grace of faith as a work, but Christ and his righteousness, the object of faith; the believer justified by the imputed righteousness of Christ, received by faith. The witnesses draw the completeness of this justification: "justified from all things" — Gill notes it is complete and perfect, from all sin (original and actual, secret and open, of every kind), from all things the law can charge as breaches of it, from all things the justice of God can demand satisfaction for; the full and perfect justification, leaving no sin uncovered, no charge unanswered. The expositors observe the contrast with the law: "from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses" — the law of Moses could not justify; the law condemns, but cannot justify; it demands obedience, but provides no righteousness for the guilty; the sinner cannot be justified by the law, which only convicts him; "by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified" (Romans 3:20; Galatians 2:16). The expositors note the importance of this for Paul's hearers: to the Jews, trusting in the law, Paul declares that the law cannot justify, but Christ can; the great announcement that justification is by faith, not by the law; the gospel's answer to the works of the law. The witnesses draw the lesson on free and complete justification: the believer marks justification by faith in Christ, complete and perfect from all things, which the law could never give; the great doctrine of the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests his justification on Christ alone, received by faith, complete from all things; not on the law, which cannot justify. The verse thus records the doctrine of justification by faith — all believers justified by Christ from all things, which the law could not do — teaching free and complete justification by faith in Christ, not by the works of the law.
40:
"Beware therefore, lest that come upon you, which is spoken of in the prophets;" The witnesses note Paul's warning against rejecting the gospel. Gill notes the warning is against "rejecting the Gospel, and those excellent truths of it; since forgiveness of sin and a justifying righteousness are said to be had in no other way, but in and through Christ." Barnes notes Paul cautioned them lest the threatened punishment come on them, "as the Jews held that they were safe"; the warning the more needful for their false security. The witnesses note Paul warning the hearers to beware lest the judgment foretold by the prophets fall on them — the solemn caution against despising the gospel of forgiveness and justification. The witnesses note the warning. The witnesses note the danger of rejecting.

The witnesses note Paul's solemn warning — beware lest the judgment of the prophets fall on those who reject the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's warning against rejecting the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the solemn caution against despising the gospel of forgiveness and justification, lest the foretold judgment fall. The witnesses note the warning: "Beware therefore, lest that come upon you, which is spoken of in the prophets" — Paul, having preached forgiveness and justification through Christ, warns the hearers to beware lest the judgment foretold by the prophets come upon them; the solemn caution following the gracious offer. The expositors observe the ground of the warning, which Gill marks: the warning is against rejecting the gospel and its excellent truths — for forgiveness of sin and a justifying righteousness are to be had no other way than through Christ; to reject the gospel is to reject the only way of salvation, and so to incur judgment. The witnesses press the need of the warning, which Barnes notes: the warning was the more needful because the Jews held themselves safe — secure in their descent from Abraham, their possession of the law; their false security making the caution necessary; the warning addressed to those who thought themselves in no danger. The expositors draw the lesson on the danger of rejecting the gospel: to despise the gospel of forgiveness and justification is to incur judgment; the rejection of Christ, the only Saviour, leaving no other way; the warning that the threatened judgment falls on the despisers. The witnesses observe the balance of the gospel offer and the warning: Paul offers forgiveness and justification freely (v.38-39), then warns against rejecting it; the offer and the warning together; the grace held out, the danger of refusing it declared; the gospel both gracious and solemn. The expositors note the source of the warning "in the prophets": Paul cites the prophets (Habakkuk 1:5, the next verse) — the warning grounded in Scripture; the judgment on despisers foretold of old, applied to the hearers; the prophetic word the ground of the caution. The witnesses draw the lesson on heeding the warning: the believer marks the danger of rejecting the gospel, the false security of those who think themselves safe, and the solemn warning of judgment on despisers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Paul's warning, the danger of despising the gospel, and the need to heed the offer of forgiveness lest judgment fall. The verse thus records Paul's warning against rejecting the gospel — beware lest the judgment of the prophets fall — teaching the solemn caution against despising the gospel of forgiveness and justification.
41:
"Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish: for I work a work in your days, a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you." The witnesses note the prophetic warning to the despisers. Gill discusses the rendering of "despisers" (Habakkuk 1:5; the Septuagint "despisers," the Hebrew variously "among the heathen"); the Syriac "ye impudent," the Arabic "ye negligent." The witnesses note Paul citing Habakkuk's warning — the despisers to wonder and perish, confronted with a work of God they would not believe; applied to those who would reject the gospel and the work of God in Christ. The witnesses note the warning to despisers. The witnesses note the work not believed.

The witnesses note the warning to despisers — to wonder and perish before a work of God they would not believe.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophetic warning to the despisers. The lesson the expositors draw is the solemn warning that the despisers of God's work shall wonder and perish, applied to those who reject the gospel. The witnesses note the citation: "Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish" — Paul cites Habakkuk 1:5; Gill discusses the rendering (the Septuagint "despisers," which Paul follows; the Hebrew variously "behold ye among the heathen"; the Syriac "ye impudent," the Arabic "ye negligent"); the warning to the despisers to wonder and perish. The expositors observe the original setting and the application: in Habakkuk, the warning was of the Chaldean invasion — a work of God the people would not believe; Paul applies it to the work of God in Christ and the gospel — a work the despisers would not believe, and at which they would wonder and perish. The witnesses press the meaning of the warning: "wonder, and perish" — the despisers, confronted with God's work, would be astonished and yet perish; their unbelief leading to their ruin; the work of God a savour of death to those who reject it. The expositors draw the lesson on the work not believed: "a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you" — God's work in Christ (the incarnation, death, resurrection, the gospel of free justification) was so wonderful that the despisers would not believe it, even when plainly declared; the unbelief of those who reject the gospel, refusing to believe though it is told them. The witnesses observe the application to the hearers: Paul warns the Jews that, as despisers of God's work, they were in danger of wondering and perishing — refusing to believe the gospel declared to them; the warning of Habakkuk fulfilled in those who reject Christ; the despisers of the gospel perishing in their unbelief. The expositors note the solemnity of the warning: to despise the work of God in the gospel is to perish; the despisers, confronted with the wonderful work of redemption, refusing to believe, are warned of ruin; the gospel rejected bringing judgment. The witnesses draw the lesson on the peril of unbelief: the believer marks the warning to despisers, the work of God not believed, and the ruin of those who reject the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the warning to the despisers, the wonderful work of God in the gospel, and the peril of refusing to believe it. The verse thus records the prophetic warning to the despisers — wonder and perish at a work ye would not believe — teaching the solemn warning that the despisers of God's work in the gospel shall perish in their unbelief.
42:
"And when the Jews were gone out of the synagogue, the Gentiles besought that these words might be preached to them the next sabbath." The witnesses note the eager desire for a second hearing. Chrysostom marks Paul's wisdom: "he not only gained admiration at the time, but put into them a longing desire for a second hearing, while in what he said he dropped some seeds... and forbore to solve the questions, his plan being to interest them and engage their good-will, and not make people listless by casting all at once into their minds"; the eagerness great, the people longing to hear more. The witnesses note the hearers — the Gentiles especially — begging that these words be preached again the next sabbath; the eager desire stirred by the sermon. The witnesses note the desire for a second hearing. The witnesses note the eager response.

The witnesses note the eager desire for a second hearing — the sermon stirring a longing to hear more, especially among the Gentiles.

So the witnesses make the verse the eager desire for a second hearing. The lesson the expositors draw is the longing the sermon stirred, and the wisdom that left the hearers desiring more. The witnesses note the request: "the Gentiles besought that these words might be preached to them the next sabbath" — the hearers, especially the Gentiles, begged that the same words be preached again the next sabbath; the eager desire stirred by the sermon, the longing to hear more. The expositors observe Chrysostom's marking of Paul's wisdom: Paul not only gained admiration at the time, but put into the hearers a longing for a second hearing; in his sermon he dropped some seeds, and forbore to solve all the questions or follow out the subject to its conclusion; his plan to interest them and engage their good-will, not to make them listless by casting all at once into their minds; the wisdom that leaves the hearers desiring more. The witnesses press the eagerness: "the next sabbath" — they could not wait, but begged to hear again; the hunger for the word stirred; the eager response to the gospel, the desire for further teaching. The expositors draw the lesson on the right effect of preaching: good preaching stirs a longing for more — not satiety, but hunger; the hearers eager to hear again, their appetite for the word awakened; the sermon that engages and draws, leaving the hearers wanting more. The witnesses observe the wisdom of not telling all at once: Chrysostom notes Paul forbore to follow the subject to its conclusion; the wisdom of leaving something for a second hearing, engaging the hearers' desire, not overwhelming them; the teacher's skill in measuring the word to the hearers' capacity and stirring their longing. The expositors note the prominence of the Gentiles in the request: it was especially the Gentiles (the God-fearers and proselytes) who begged to hear more; the Gentiles eager for the gospel, foreshadowing the turning to the Gentiles that would follow (v.46); the readiness of the Gentiles contrasting the later envy of the Jews. The witnesses draw the lesson on the hunger for the word: the believer marks the eager desire for a second hearing, the longing the gospel stirs, and the wisdom of preaching that engages the hearers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the eager response to the sermon, the longing to hear more, and the readiness of the Gentiles. The verse thus records the eager desire for a second hearing — the Gentiles begging to hear again — teaching the longing the gospel stirs and the wisdom that leaves the hearers desiring more.
43:
"Now when the congregation was broken up, many of the Jews and religious proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas: who, speaking to them, persuaded them to continue in the grace of God." The witnesses note the converts following the apostles and the exhortation to continue. Clarke identifies "many of the Jews" (descendants of the tribes) and "religious proselytes" (heathen converted to Judaism); and explains "persuaded them to continue in the grace of God" as that they should continue to credit the gospel and receive its influence. Chrysostom marks the eagerness — they followed Paul and Barnabas. The witnesses note many Jews and proselytes following the apostles, who exhorted them to continue in the grace of God — the first converts at Antioch, urged to perseverance. The witnesses note the converts following. The witnesses note the exhortation to continue.

The witnesses note many Jews and proselytes following the apostles — exhorted to continue in the grace of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the converts following the apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is the eager response of the converts and the exhortation to perseverance in grace. The witnesses note the following: "many of the Jews and religious proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas" — many were moved by the sermon to follow the apostles; Clarke identifies them as Jews (descendants of the tribes) and religious proselytes (heathen converted to Judaism); the first fruits of the gospel at Antioch, eager to learn more. The expositors observe Chrysostom's marking of the eagerness: the people followed Paul and Barnabas; the eager response, the desire for further instruction; the converts drawn to the apostles, seeking the grace they had heard preached. The witnesses press the exhortation: "who, speaking to them, persuaded them to continue in the grace of God" — the apostles exhorted the converts to continue in the grace of God; Clarke explains, to continue to credit the gospel and receive its influence; the exhortation to perseverance, to hold fast the grace received. The expositors draw the lesson on continuing in grace: the converts are exhorted to continue — not merely to begin, but to persevere; the danger of falling away, the need of steadfastness; "continue in the grace of God" the exhortation to hold fast to the gospel, to abide in the grace received; perseverance the mark of true faith. The witnesses observe the meaning of "the grace of God": the grace of the gospel — the free favour of God in Christ, the forgiveness and justification preached; the converts to continue in this grace, holding fast the gospel, resting in the grace they had embraced; "stand fast in the grace" (cf. 1 Peter 5:12). The expositors note the pastoral care of the apostles: not content to win converts, they exhorted them to continue, establishing them in the faith; the care that follows up conversion with exhortation to perseverance; the building up of the new converts. The witnesses draw the lesson on perseverance in grace: the believer is to continue in the grace of God, holding fast the gospel, persevering in the faith; the converts' calling to steadfastness; the exhortation a pattern for establishing new believers. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the eager following of the converts, the exhortation to continue in grace, and the calling to perseverance. The verse thus records the converts following the apostles — exhorted to continue in the grace of God — teaching the eager response of the converts and the exhortation to perseverance in grace.
44:
"And the next sabbath day came almost the whole city together to hear the word of God." The witnesses note the great concourse to hear the gospel. Barnes notes the news of the apostles' presence and doctrine had been circulated by the Gentiles who heard them, and "curiosity attracted the multitude to hear them"; almost the whole city gathered. Clarke notes the good news had spread far and wide. The witnesses note that, on the next sabbath, almost the whole city — Jews and Gentiles — came together to hear the word of God; the remarkable interest stirred by the gospel, the multitude gathered. The witnesses note the great concourse. The witnesses note the whole city gathered.

The witnesses note almost the whole city gathering to hear the word — the remarkable interest the gospel stirred.

So the witnesses make the verse the great concourse to hear the gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the remarkable interest the gospel stirred, drawing almost the whole city to hear. The witnesses note the gathering: "the next sabbath day came almost the whole city together to hear the word of God" — on the sabbath following Paul's sermon, almost the whole city — Jews and Gentiles — gathered to hear the word of God; the multitude drawn by the report of the gospel. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the cause: the news of the apostles' presence and doctrine had been circulated by the Gentiles who heard the first sermon, and curiosity attracted the multitude; the report spreading (Clarke notes, far and wide), drawing the great concourse; the gospel stirring a city-wide interest. The witnesses press the remarkable interest: "almost the whole city" — not a few, but nearly all the city; the gospel drawing an extraordinary multitude; the interest in the word of God remarkable, the city gathered to hear. The expositors draw the lesson on the drawing power of the gospel: the gospel, preached with power, drew almost the whole city; the word of God attracting the multitude; the remarkable interest a token of the gospel's reach and the people's hunger. The witnesses observe the substance of their gathering: "to hear the word of God" — they came to hear the word, the gospel; the multitude assembled for the preaching; the word of God the attraction, the gospel the draw. The expositors note the contrast this gathering would occasion: the great concourse, especially of Gentiles, would provoke the envy of the Jews (the next verse); the very success of the gospel stirring the opposition; the multitude that drew the Gentiles drew also the Jews' envy. The witnesses observe the providence in the great gathering: the gospel reaching almost the whole city, the word spreading, the multitude hearing; the providence drawing the people to hear, that many might believe (v.48); the great concourse the occasion of a great harvest and a great opposition. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gospel's drawing power: the believer marks the remarkable interest the gospel stirred, the great concourse to hear, and the providence drawing the multitude. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks almost the whole city gathered, the interest the gospel stirred, and the multitude drawn to the word. The verse thus records the great concourse to hear the gospel — almost the whole city gathered — teaching the remarkable interest the gospel stirred and the multitude drawn to hear the word of God.
45:
"But when the Jews saw the multitudes, they were filled with envy, and spake against those things which were spoken by Paul, contradicting and blaspheming." The witnesses note the Jews' envy at the great concourse. Barnes explains "envy" (Greek, zeal): "the word here denotes wrath, indignation, that such multitudes should be disposed to hear a message which they rejected, and which threatened to overthrow their religion"; they spake against Paul's doctrine, contradicting and blaspheming — reviling Jesus of Nazareth. The witnesses note the unbelieving Jews, seeing the multitudes drawn to the gospel, filled with envy, opposing Paul's preaching with contradiction and blasphemy. The witnesses note the Jews' envy. The witnesses note the opposition.

The witnesses note the Jews filled with envy at the multitudes — opposing the gospel with contradiction and blasphemy.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jews' envy at the gospel's success. The lesson the expositors draw is the envy that moved the Jews against the gospel and the blasphemy of their opposition. The witnesses note the occasion: "when the Jews saw the multitudes, they were filled with envy" — the unbelieving Jews, seeing the great multitude (especially of Gentiles) drawn to hear the gospel, were filled with envy; Barnes notes the word denotes wrath and indignation that such multitudes should be disposed to hear a message they rejected; the envy at the gospel's success. The expositors observe the root of the opposition: it was envy — the galling sight of the multitudes drawn to Paul, the people turning to the gospel, their own influence eclipsed; the envy of those who saw the gospel prevailing and their religion threatened; "for envy they had delivered" Christ (Matthew 27:18), and for envy they now opposed his apostles. The witnesses press the form of the opposition: "spake against those things which were spoken by Paul, contradicting and blaspheming" — they contradicted Paul's doctrine, opposing his preaching; and blasphemed, reviling Jesus of Nazareth, speaking of him with contempt; the opposition rising to blasphemy against Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the envy that opposes the gospel: the success of the gospel provokes the envy of its enemies; the more the gospel prevails, the more its opponents are stirred; the envy of those who will not believe and cannot bear that others should; the root of much opposition to the gospel. The witnesses observe the contrast with the multitudes: the multitudes came eagerly to hear (v.44), but the Jews were filled with envy; the same gospel drawing the hungry and provoking the envious; the difference in the heart. The expositors note the sin of the blasphemy: their contradiction passed into blasphemy — reviling Christ; the opposition to the gospel issuing in railing against the Lord; the sin of speaking against the Saviour. The witnesses draw the lesson on the opposition the gospel meets: the believer marks the envy that moves the enemies of the gospel, the opposition its success provokes, and the blasphemy of those who reject Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jews' envy at the multitudes, the opposition to the gospel, and the blasphemy of its enemies. The verse thus records the Jews' envy at the gospel's success — opposing with contradiction and blasphemy — teaching the envy that moves the enemies of the gospel and the sin of their opposition.
46:
"Then Paul and Barnabas waxed bold, and said, It was necessary that the word of God should first have been spoken to you: but seeing ye put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life, lo, we turn to the Gentiles." The witnesses note the bold turning to the Gentiles. Barnes notes the apostles "waxed bold" — not terrified by the opposition; "the contradictions and blasphemies of sinners often show that their consciences are alarmed... then is not the time to shrink, but to declare more fearlessly the truth"; it was "necessary" — commanded — to offer the gospel first to the Jews (Luke 24:47); but they, rejecting it, judged themselves unworthy. The witnesses note Paul declaring the gospel offered first to the Jews, but, they rejecting it, turning to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the bold turning. The witnesses note the gospel to the Jew first.

The witnesses note the bold turning to the Gentiles — the gospel offered first to the Jews, who put it from them.

So the witnesses make the verse the bold turning to the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is the order of the gospel (to the Jew first), the self-condemnation of the rejecters, and the turning to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the boldness: "Paul and Barnabas waxed bold" — the apostles, far from being terrified by the opposition, grew bolder; Barnes notes that the contradictions and blasphemies of sinners often show their consciences are alarmed, the truth taking effect; and then is not the time to shrink, but to declare the truth more fearlessly; the boldness that rises with opposition. The expositors observe the order of the gospel: "It was necessary that the word of God should first have been spoken to you" — it was necessary, commanded, that the gospel be offered first to the Jews (Luke 24:47, "beginning at Jerusalem"; Romans 1:16, "to the Jew first"); the Jews having the first claim, the gospel offered to them first; the divine order of the gospel's advance. The witnesses press the self-condemnation of the rejecters: "but seeing ye put it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of everlasting life" — the Jews, rejecting the gospel, put it from them, and thereby judged themselves unworthy of everlasting life; their own rejection the verdict against themselves; not that God judged them unworthy, but that, by refusing the gospel, they declared themselves unworthy; the self-condemnation of unbelief. The expositors draw the lesson on rejecting the gospel: to reject the gospel is to judge oneself unworthy of eternal life — to refuse the offered salvation, to condemn oneself by one's own act; the rejecter sealing his own doom; the gospel refused leaving the soul self-condemned. The witnesses note the turning to the Gentiles: "lo, we turn to the Gentiles" — the Jews having rejected the gospel, the apostles turn to the Gentiles; the great turning, the gospel passing to the nations; the rejection of the Jews the occasion of the Gentiles' calling (Romans 11:11). The expositors observe the providence in the turning: the Jews' rejection opened the door to the Gentiles; the gospel, refused by the Jews, offered to the nations; the providence overruling the Jews' unbelief to advance the gospel to the Gentiles. The witnesses draw the lesson on the order and the turning: the believer marks the gospel offered first to the Jews, the self-condemnation of the rejecters, and the turning to the Gentiles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bold turning to the Gentiles, the order of the gospel, and the self-condemnation of those who reject it. The verse thus records the bold turning to the Gentiles — the gospel offered first to the Jews, who put it from them — teaching the order of the gospel, the self-condemnation of the rejecters, and the turning to the Gentiles.
47:
"For so hath the Lord commanded us, saying, I have set thee to be a light of the Gentiles, that thou shouldest be for salvation unto the ends of the earth." The witnesses note the scriptural warrant for the turning to the Gentiles. Barnes notes Paul "appeals to the Scriptures to justify his course" — and to the Old Testament (Isaiah 49:6) rather than to Christ's command, because the Jews recognized their own Scriptures; the passage refers to the Messiah and his mission to the Gentiles. Gill connects it also to Christ's commission to preach to all nations. The witnesses note Paul grounding the turning to the Gentiles on Isaiah 49:6 — Christ (and his apostles) set to be a light to the Gentiles, salvation to the ends of the earth. The witnesses note the scriptural warrant. The witnesses note the light to the Gentiles.

The witnesses note the scriptural warrant — Christ set to be a light to the Gentiles, salvation to the ends of the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the scriptural warrant for the Gentile mission. The lesson the expositors draw is that the turning to the Gentiles was commanded and foretold in Scripture, Christ set to be a light to the nations. The witnesses note the warrant: "For so hath the Lord commanded us" — Paul grounds the turning to the Gentiles on the Lord's command, given in Scripture; the mission to the Gentiles not the apostles' invention but God's command. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the appeal to the Old Testament: Paul appeals to the Scriptures (Isaiah 49:6) rather than to Christ's command, because the Jews recognized the authority of their own Scriptures, while they would have scorned the command of Jesus of Nazareth; the wisdom of grounding the Gentile mission in the prophets the Jews revered. The witnesses press the citation: "I have set thee to be a light of the Gentiles, that thou shouldest be for salvation unto the ends of the earth" (Isaiah 49:6) — the Messiah set to be a light to the Gentiles, salvation to the ends of the earth; the mission to the Gentiles foretold in the prophet, the Messiah's work embracing the nations. The expositors draw the lesson on the Gentile mission foretold: the turning to the Gentiles was no afterthought, but foretold in Scripture; the Messiah ordained to be a light to the nations, salvation to the ends of the earth; the gospel's reach to the Gentiles part of the prophetic vision. The witnesses observe the application to the apostles: "the Lord commanded us" — the apostles, as Christ's ministers, share in the mission; the light to the Gentiles, given in Christ, carried by his apostles; Gill connects it also to Christ's commission to preach to all nations (Matthew 28:19); the apostles fulfilling the prophecy by carrying the gospel to the Gentiles. The expositors note the breadth of the mission: "unto the ends of the earth" — the gospel for all nations, to the farthest reaches; the universal scope of the Gentile mission; the salvation of Christ for the whole world. The witnesses draw the lesson on the foretold and commanded Gentile mission: the believer marks the scriptural warrant for the turning to the Gentiles, Christ the light to the nations, and the gospel's reach to the ends of the earth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the command and the prophecy, the light to the Gentiles, and the universal mission of the gospel. The verse thus records the scriptural warrant for the Gentile mission — Christ set to be a light to the Gentiles — teaching that the turning to the Gentiles was commanded and foretold, the gospel for the ends of the earth.
48:
"And when the Gentiles heard this, they were glad, and glorified the word of the Lord: and as many as were ordained to eternal life believed." The witnesses note the Gentiles' joy and the famous statement on ordination to life. Barnes notes the Gentiles rejoiced that salvation was no longer confined to the Jews, and "glorified the word of the Lord," honouring it as from God. On "as many as were ordained to eternal life believed," Barnes notes the Greek (and the Syriac "destined," Vulgate) — the natural reading of divine ordination; though the precise sense has been debated. The witnesses note the Gentiles glad at the gospel offered them, glorifying the word, and the believing connected with ordination to eternal life. The witnesses note the Gentiles' joy. The witnesses note ordination to life.

The witnesses note the Gentiles' joy and the believing of as many as were ordained to eternal life.

So the witnesses make the verse the Gentiles' joy and the ordination to life. The lesson the expositors draw is the gladness of the Gentiles at the gospel and the relation of believing to God's ordination to eternal life. The witnesses note the joy: "when the Gentiles heard this, they were glad, and glorified the word of the Lord" — the Gentiles, hearing that the gospel was offered to them, rejoiced; Barnes notes they were glad that salvation was no longer confined to the Jews, and glorified the word of the Lord, honouring and receiving it as from God; the joy of the Gentiles at their inclusion. The expositors observe the reception of the word: the Gentiles "glorified the word of the Lord" — receiving it with reverence and gladness, as the word of God; the right reception of the gospel, honouring it as divine; the contrast with the Jews who blasphemed (v.45). The witnesses note the famous statement: "as many as were ordained to eternal life believed" — those who believed were as many as were ordained to eternal life; the believing connected with God's ordination to life. The expositors weigh the sense, which has been much debated. The natural and common reading (Barnes notes the Greek, the Syriac "destined," the Vulgate) is of divine ordination or appointment — those whom God had ordained, appointed, or destined to eternal life believed; the believing the fruit of God's electing ordination; the Reformed reading, that election to life issues in faith ("whom he did predestinate, them he also called," Romans 8:30; "ordained to eternal life"). The witnesses note the alternative readings some have offered — that "ordained" means "disposed" or "inclined" (those disposed to eternal life believed); yet the expositors observe that the word and the natural sense point to divine ordination, not merely human disposition; the text most plainly teaching that the believing flowed from God's ordination to life. The expositors draw the lesson on election and faith: the faith of the Gentiles is traced to God's ordination — as many as were ordained to life believed; the believing the fruit and evidence of God's electing grace; salvation of God, the faith of the elect the working out of his ordination. The witnesses observe the comfort and the humility this teaches: the believer's faith is the fruit of God's ordination to life; salvation begun and secured in God's purpose; the glory of salvation God's, the faith his gift. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Gentiles' joy, the believing of as many as were ordained to life, and the relation of faith to God's electing grace. The verse thus records the Gentiles' joy and the ordination to life — as many as were ordained to eternal life believed — teaching the gladness of the Gentiles and the believing that flowed from God's ordination to eternal life.
49:
"And the word of the Lord was published throughout all the region." The witnesses note the spread of the gospel through the region. Gill notes the word was published throughout all the region of Pisidia, not by Paul and Barnabas (who went on to Iconium), but by the new converts gifted to preach; many churches were planted, and that at Antioch continued for centuries. The witnesses note the gospel, having taken root at Antioch, spreading throughout the surrounding region — the word published widely, the new converts carrying it on; the gospel's expansion from the city to the country. The witnesses note the gospel published. The witnesses note the regional spread.

The witnesses note the gospel published throughout the region — spreading from Antioch through the surrounding country.

So the witnesses make the verse the spread of the gospel through the region. The lesson the expositors draw is the expansion of the gospel from the city to the surrounding region, carried on by the new converts. The witnesses note the spread: "the word of the Lord was published throughout all the region" — the gospel, having taken root at Antioch, spread throughout the surrounding region of Pisidia; the word published widely, beyond the city, through the country. The expositors observe Gill's note on the means: the word was published not by Paul and Barnabas (who went on to Iconium, v.51), but by the new converts, gifted to preach the gospel to others; the converts becoming evangelists, carrying the word through the region; the gospel spreading by the labour of the new believers. The witnesses press the fruit of the Antioch mission: many churches were planted in the region; the gospel taking root and spreading; Gill notes the church at Antioch in Pisidia continued for centuries (a patriarchate and metropolitan in later ages); the lasting fruit of the mission. The expositors draw the lesson on the expansion of the gospel: from the city of Antioch, the gospel spread through the whole region; the word published widely, the new converts carrying it on; the gospel's reach extending from the center to the country, the harvest multiplying. The witnesses observe the providence in the spread: the gospel, opposed by the Jews at Antioch, yet spread through the region; the opposition not stopping but, in providence, accompanying the gospel's advance; the word published despite the enemies. The expositors note the role of the converts in the spread: the new believers, gifted and zealous, carried the gospel through the region; the gospel spread not by the apostles only, but by the converts; every believer a witness, the word published by many. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gospel's expansion: the believer marks the spread of the gospel through the region, the converts carrying it on, and the lasting fruit of the mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the word published throughout the region, the converts' labour, and the gospel's expansion from the city to the country. The verse thus records the spread of the gospel through the region — the word published throughout all Pisidia — teaching the expansion of the gospel from the city to the surrounding region, carried on by the new converts.
50:
"But the Jews stirred up the devout and honourable women, and the chief men of the city, and raised persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and expelled them out of their coasts." The witnesses note the Jews stirring up persecution. Gill identifies the "devout and honourable women" as Gentile women proselyted to the Jewish religion, religious and of figure and distinction, whose husbands were chief men of the city; the Jews working through them upon their husbands. The witnesses note the unbelieving Jews, unable to answer the gospel, stirring up the influential women and the chief men to raise persecution against the apostles and expel them — opposition turning to persecution. The witnesses note the persecution stirred up. The witnesses note the expulsion.

The witnesses note the Jews stirring up the influential to persecute the apostles — opposition turning to expulsion.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jews stirring up persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the malice that, unable to answer the gospel, resorts to persecution through the influential. The witnesses note the means: "the Jews stirred up the devout and honourable women, and the chief men of the city" — the unbelieving Jews, unable to refute the gospel, stirred up the influential — the devout and honourable women (Gill notes, Gentile women proselyted to Judaism, religious and of distinction, whose husbands were chief men) and the chief men of the city; working through the influential to raise opposition. The expositors observe the craft of the malice: the Jews, defeated in argument and envious of the gospel's success, resorted to stirring up the powerful — the prominent women and the city's leaders; using their influence to raise persecution; the malice that, lacking truth, employs power and influence. The witnesses press the persecution: "raised persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and expelled them out of their coasts" — the opposition turned to persecution, the apostles driven out of the region; the malice issuing in expulsion; the gospel's messengers persecuted and banished. The expositors draw the lesson on the malice of the enemies: unable to answer the gospel, they persecute its preachers; the opposition that cannot refute the truth resorts to force; the apostles persecuted not for any wrong, but for preaching the gospel; the world's enmity against the truth. The witnesses observe the irony of the "devout" women stirred up: those called "devout and honourable," religious in their way, were stirred up to persecute the apostles; religion without truth made the instrument of persecution; the zeal of the misguided turned against the gospel; "they shall put you out of the synagogues... whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service" (John 16:2). The expositors note the use of influence in the persecution: the Jews worked through the prominent women and the chief men; the persecution organized through the influential; the powers of the city turned against the apostles. The witnesses draw the lesson on persecution for the gospel: the believer marks the malice that persecutes what it cannot answer, the use of influence against the gospel, and the expulsion of the apostles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the persecution stirred up, the malice of the enemies, and the apostles driven out for the gospel. The verse thus records the Jews stirring up persecution — raising it through the influential and expelling the apostles — teaching the malice that, unable to answer the gospel, resorts to persecution.
51:
"But they shook off the dust of their feet against them, and came unto Iconium." The witnesses note the apostles' symbolic act in departing. Clarke explains the rite: "this was a very significant rite; by it they in effect said, Ye are worse than the heathen: even your very land is accursed for your opposition to God, and we dare not permit even its dust to cleave to the soles of our feet"; done according to Christ's command (Matthew 10:14), "for a testimony against you, that we offered you salvation, but ye rejected it." The witnesses note the apostles shaking off the dust of their feet against the persecutors and going to Iconium — the testimony against those who rejected the gospel. The witnesses note the shaking off the dust. The witnesses note the testimony against them.

The witnesses note the apostles shaking off the dust against the persecutors — a testimony against those who rejected the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' symbolic act in departing. The lesson the expositors draw is the solemn testimony against those who rejected the gospel, signified by shaking off the dust. The witnesses note the act: "they shook off the dust of their feet against them" — the apostles, departing, shook the dust of the city from their feet against the persecutors; the symbolic act of testimony, done according to Christ's command (Matthew 10:14). The expositors observe Clarke's explanation of the rite: it was a significant act, by which they in effect said, Ye are worse than the heathen; your very land is accursed for your opposition to God, and we dare not let even its dust cleave to our feet; the Jews themselves shook off the dust when leaving heathen lands, and now the apostles treat the rejecting Jews as worse than the heathen; the act a solemn renunciation. The witnesses press the testimony it bore: done "for a testimony against you, that we offered you salvation, but ye rejected it" (cf. Mark 6:11; Luke 9:5) — the act a witness against the rejecters, that the gospel had been offered and refused; the testimony that the apostles had done their part, the guilt of rejection on the rejecters; the dust shaken off declaring their responsibility ended and the rejecters' guilt sealed. The expositors draw the lesson on the solemnity of rejecting the gospel: to reject the gospel is to incur this testimony — the messengers shaking off the dust, the guilt on the rejecters; the solemn act marking the seriousness of refusing the offered salvation; the rejecters left without excuse. The witnesses observe the apostles' obedience to Christ's command: they did as Christ had directed (Matthew 10:14), shaking off the dust; the obedience to the Lord's instruction even in departing; the act commanded, not invented. The expositors note the apostles' moving on: "and came unto Iconium" — they did not stay to contend, but moved on to the next field; the gospel carried elsewhere, the rejecters left to their testimony; the apostles undeterred, advancing to Iconium. The witnesses draw the lesson on the testimony against rejecters: the believer marks the solemn act of shaking off the dust, the testimony against those who reject the gospel, and the guilt sealed on the rejecters. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostles' obedience, the testimony against the persecutors, and the moving on to the next field. The verse thus records the apostles shaking off the dust and going to Iconium — a testimony against the rejecters — teaching the solemn testimony against those who reject the gospel and the apostles' advance to the next field.
52:
"And the disciples were filled with joy, and with the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the joy of the disciples amid persecution. Gill takes "the disciples" as the new converts at Antioch (or perhaps the apostles), filled with joy at the gospel and at being counted worthy to suffer; and notes the joy and the Holy Ghost joined. Barnes marks the wonder: "this happened even in the midst of persecution, and is one of the many evidences that the gospel is able to fill the soul with joy, even in the severest trials." The witnesses note the disciples, despite the persecution that drove out the apostles, filled with joy and the Holy Ghost — the joy of the gospel triumphing over trial. The witnesses note the disciples' joy. The witnesses note joy in persecution.

The witnesses note the disciples filled with joy and the Holy Ghost — the gospel's joy triumphing even amid persecution.

So the witnesses make the verse the joy of the disciples amid persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the joy and the Spirit's fullness that filled the disciples even in the midst of persecution. The witnesses note the joy: "the disciples were filled with joy, and with the Holy Ghost" — the disciples (Gill takes them as the new converts at Antioch, or perhaps the apostles) were filled with joy and with the Holy Ghost; the joy of the gospel and the fullness of the Spirit, even after the apostles had been persecuted and expelled. The expositors observe Barnes's marking of the wonder: this joy happened even in the midst of persecution; one of the many evidences that the gospel is able to fill the soul with joy even in the severest trials; the joy not hindered but heightened by the persecution; the gospel's joy triumphing over trial. The witnesses press the union of joy and the Holy Ghost: "filled with joy, and with the Holy Ghost" — the joy flowing from the Spirit; "the fruit of the Spirit is... joy" (Galatians 5:22); the Holy Ghost the source of the joy; the disciples filled with the Spirit, and so with joy; the joy a fruit of the Spirit's fullness. The expositors draw the lesson on joy in persecution: the disciples, though their teachers were driven out and the persecution raged, were filled with joy; the gospel's joy independent of circumstances, triumphing over trial; "rejoicing in tribulation" (Romans 12:12); the joy that the world cannot give nor take away. The witnesses observe the source of this joy: the Holy Ghost — the joy not of nature, nor of circumstance, but of the Spirit; the disciples filled with the Spirit, and so with a joy that persecution could not quench; the supernatural joy of the gospel. The expositors note the triumphant close of the chapter: the chapter that ends with persecution and expulsion ends also with joy and the Holy Ghost; the opposition failing to quench the joy; the gospel advancing, the disciples rejoicing; the triumph of the gospel over its enemies. The witnesses draw the lesson on the joy of the gospel: the believer marks the disciples' joy amid persecution, the joy as a fruit of the Spirit, and the gospel's joy triumphing over trial. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the joy and the Spirit's fullness, the joy that persecution cannot quench, and the triumph of the gospel's joy over trial. The verse thus records the disciples filled with joy and the Holy Ghost — even amid persecution — teaching the gospel's joy, the fruit of the Spirit, triumphing over trial.
